A large, rigorous trial found no difference between real ear acupuncture, sham acupuncture, and relaxation in reducing cocaine use or improving treatment retention [3]. Other controlled studies similarly found no clear benefit of true ear acupuncture over placebo or standard psychosocial care [2,4–7]. While one small study suggested benefit [1], the overall evidence indicates ear acupuncture should not replace proven addiction treatments.

- **Rationale**:  
One randomized clinical trial compared people having cataract surgery as day cases with those staying in hospital overnight [1]. It measured vision, complications, patients’ own ratings of vision and health, and costs. Vision and overall health results were the same in both groups, both soon after surgery and at 4 months [1]. Day-surgery patients had more minor complications in the first 24 hours, but by 4 months there was no difference in complication rates, suggesting no lasting harm [1]. The cost per operation was clearly lower for day surgery than for inpatient care [1]. A second study of 250 patients found that doing cataract surgery entirely on an outpatient basis, with early walking and follow-up outside hospital, did not reduce the success of surgery [2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Day surgery for cataract is as effective as staying in hospital, with similar vision, long-term safety, and patient-reported outcomes, while costing less overall [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how effective “day surgery” (outpatient/ambulatory surgery) is for cataract compared with staying in hospital. The trials compare visual outcomes, complications, patient‑reported health, and costs. Both studies show similar surgical success and long‑term vision with day surgery versus inpatient care. One trial finds slightly more early (first 24 hours) minor complications in outpatients, but no difference in long‑term outcomes. Both studies note that hospitalization is not needed for good results and that outpatient care is cheaper. So, for most people, day cataract surgery is highly effective, safe, and more cost‑effective.

- **Full Answer**:  
Day (outpatient) cataract surgery appears to be as effective and safe as staying in hospital for the operation for most people. In a large randomized trial comparing almost 500 day-surgery patients with a similar number of inpatients, there were no meaningful differences in vision after surgery, overall eye health, or patients’ ratings of their visual function and general health four months later [1]. Day-surgery patients did have more minor complications in the first 24 hours (such as temporary swelling or pressure issues), but these early problems did not affect long‑term results [1]. Another study of 250 cataract patients found that the success of cataract extraction was not reduced when people walked around soon after surgery and had all their aftercare as outpatients [2]. Because day surgery also costs less than inpatient care, it is considered a safe, effective, and more cost‑effective option for routine cataract surgery [1].

- **Rationale**:  
Research directly compared cataract surgery done as day surgery (outpatient) versus staying in hospital (inpatient). In a large randomized trial, vision and overall health results were the same in both groups a few months after surgery, meaning that going home the same day did not reduce the success of the operation [1]. There were more minor early complications (within 24 hours) in the day-surgery group, but by four months these differences had disappeared, so long-term outcomes were unaffected [1]. Another study of 250 patients also found that cataract surgery remained successful when patients walked soon after surgery and all aftercare was done outside the hospital [2]. Both studies reported that outpatient care can lower costs compared with hospital stays [1,2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Cataract day surgery works well: it provides similar vision and health outcomes to inpatient care, with lower costs, though it needs good follow-up to manage slightly more early minor issues [1,2].

- **Rationale**:  
Research directly compares cataract operations done as day (outpatient) surgery with those requiring a hospital stay. One randomized trial found no difference in vision or overall eye health four months after surgery between outpatient and inpatient groups, meaning effectiveness was the same [1]. Early problems in the first 24 hours were somewhat more frequent in day-surgery patients, but these issues did not lead to worse longer-term outcomes [1]. Day surgery also clearly reduced costs [1]. A separate study of 250 patients likewise reported that doing cataract surgery fully on an outpatient basis, with early walking and home recovery, did not reduce the success of the procedure [2]. Together, these studies show that for people who are suitable candidates, day cataract surgery is safe, effective, and cheaper than staying in hospital.

- **Full Answer**:  
Day (outpatient) cataract surgery appears to work just as well as staying overnight in hospital for most people.

In a large trial comparing 464 day-surgery patients with 471 inpatients, final vision and overall eye health were the same in both groups four months after surgery [1]. Early, minor complications in the first 24 hours (such as temporary pressure changes or minor inflammation) were a bit more common in day-surgery patients, but these differences disappeared over time and did not affect long‑term results [1]. Day surgery also cost less than inpatient care (about 1001 vs 1218 Euros), making it more cost‑effective [1].

Another study of 250 people found that doing cataract surgery entirely on an outpatient basis, with patients walking soon after surgery and recovering at home, did not reduce the success of the operation [2].

Overall, evidence supports day cataract surgery as safe, effective, and less expensive for suitable patients.

- **Rationale**:  
Both studies looked at very premature babies with serious early lung disease. They tested whether the antibiotic erythromycin could prevent or lessen chronic lung problems, especially when a germ called *Ureaplasma urealyticum* was present. In the first trial, babies were randomly given erythromycin or no antibiotic. The medicine did not lower lung inflammation, did not reduce how many babies developed chronic lung disease, and did not make the disease milder [1]. In the second study, erythromycin did clear the *Ureaplasma* germ in most treated babies, but this did not shorten the time they needed extra oxygen or improve long‑term breathing outcomes [2]. Other factors, such as being more premature, heart vessel problems, and infections, were more strongly linked to long‑lasting lung problems [2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
In these studies, antibiotics (erythromycin) were not very effective for long‑lasting lung problems in premature infants.  

Giving erythromycin from birth did not reduce how often chronic lung disease developed or how severe it was [1].  

In babies colonized with *Ureaplasma*, erythromycin successfully removed the germ in most cases, but it did not reduce the time they needed oxygen or improve overall long‑term breathing outcomes [2].  

Long‑term lung problems were more closely linked to how early the baby was born and other complications like certain heart issues and later infections, rather than to this specific germ [2].  

So, while erythromycin can clear some bacteria from the lungs, the available evidence shows it does not meaningfully prevent or improve chronic lung disease in these high‑risk premature infants [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
Both studies looked at very premature babies needing breathing machines and at risk for chronic lung disease (long‑lasting lung problems). One trial randomly assigned babies to receive the antibiotic erythromycin for a week or no antibiotic; it found no difference in inflammation markers or in how often or how severely chronic lung disease developed [1]. The second study showed erythromycin successfully cleared Ureaplasma bacteria from most colonized infants, but this did not shorten oxygen treatment or improve long‑term breathing outcomes [2]. Overall, these data indicate antibiotics alone are not effective for preventing or treating this long‑lasting lung problem in such infants [1][2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
In very premature babies, giving the antibiotic erythromycin soon after birth did not reduce lung inflammation or lower the chance of developing chronic lung disease compared with no antibiotic [1]. Even when it cleared Ureaplasma bacteria, it did not lessen oxygen needs or improve long‑term breathing [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles studied very premature babies at high risk of chronic lung disease. In both trials, the antibiotic erythromycin was given early to see if it could prevent or lessen long‑term lung problems. One study found no reduction in lung inflammation or in the number of babies who developed chronic lung disease when erythromycin was used from birth [1]. The other study showed erythromycin could clear a specific germ (Ureaplasma urealyticum), but this did not shorten the time babies needed oxygen; long‑term breathing problems were more related to prematurity and other complications [2]. This supports the idea that antibiotics only help when bacteria are the main cause, not for chronic lung damage itself.

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotics only help lung problems when bacteria are a main cause. In preterm babies at high risk of chronic lung disease, the antibiotic erythromycin was tested to see if it could prevent or lessen long‑term lung damage. Giving erythromycin for 7 days from birth did not reduce lung inflammation or the number of babies who developed chronic lung disease, defined as still needing oxygen at 36 weeks’ gestation [1]. In another study, erythromycin did successfully clear a germ called Ureaplasma urealyticum from most colonized babies, but treated and untreated babies needed oxygen for the same length of time [2]. The need for long‑term oxygen was instead linked to how premature the baby was and other medical problems, not to whether they received antibiotics [2]. These results suggest that, in this setting, antibiotics do not improve long‑lasting lung problems unless there is an active bacterial infection that clearly needs treatment today.

- **Rationale**: Two studies looked at whether the antibiotic erythromycin helps prevent or improve long‑lasting lung problems (chronic lung disease) in very premature babies who needed breathing machines. One trial gave erythromycin for a week after birth and found no reduction in how often chronic lung disease developed or how severe it was [1]. Another study showed erythromycin cleared a specific germ (Ureaplasma) from the airway, but did not shorten oxygen use or improve breathing outcomes [2].

- **Full Answer**: In these studies of high‑risk premature infants, using the antibiotic erythromycin did not prevent chronic lung disease and did not make existing long‑term lung problems better, even though it reduced certain bacteria in the lungs [1][2]. This means that, for this group, antibiotics were not an effective treatment for ongoing lung damage; they only treated infection. Long‑lasting lung problems usually need other treatments such as oxygen, inhaled medicines, or breathing support, not routine antibiotics [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is broad, but the articles focus on specific chronic genital skin conditions: vulvar lichen sclerosus in women and balanitis xerotica obliterans in boys. Across several randomized trials, very potent steroid creams (especially clobetasol) are consistently more effective than hormone creams (testosterone, dihydrotestosterone) or simple placebo/cream bases for reducing symptoms and visible inflammation. One large trial found no advantage of 2% testosterone over plain petrolatum, and testosterone sometimes worsened control when used after clobetasol. Another high‑quality study confirmed clobetasol as more effective than pimecrolimus for reducing inflammation, though both helped symptoms. In boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans, a potent steroid (mometasone) improved disease especially at early stages. Because these are potent drugs and genital rashes have many different causes (including infections and STIs not covered here), it is important not to generalize blindly: effectiveness depends on correct diagnosis and supervised use.

- **Full Answer**:  
Topical creams can be very effective for certain chronic genital rashes, but results depend on the exact diagnosis and the medicine used.  

For vulvar lichen sclerosus, very strong steroid creams like 0.05% clobetasol give major relief: about three‑quarters of women had remission of itching and pain, and doctors also saw clear healing of skin damage after a few months [5]. In another trial, both clobetasol and pimecrolimus improved symptoms, but clobetasol reduced inflammation more and remains the preferred first treatment [7]. In boys with a related condition (balanitis xerotica obliterans), a potent steroid cream improved tightness and seemed to slow or stop worsening, especially in early disease [4].  

In contrast, hormone creams such as testosterone or dihydrotestosterone gave little or no extra symptom relief compared with plain ointment, and sometimes did worse than simple moisturizers [1][2][3][6].  

Because genital rashes have many causes, see a clinician first before using strong creams.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles mainly studied specific long‑lasting genital skin diseases (lichen sclerosus in women, balanitis xerotica obliterans in boys), not every type of genital rash. They compared different prescription creams (strong steroids, hormone creams, immune creams) and sometimes plain moisturizing cream. Strong steroid creams repeatedly showed the best results for both symptoms and skin appearance, while hormone creams (like testosterone) often worked no better than simple ointment and sometimes made things worse after steroid treatment. This suggests that, when the rash is due to certain inflammatory skin conditions, the *right* prescribed topical cream can be very effective, but not all creams work, and diagnosis matters.

- **Full Answer**:  
For some chronic genital skin conditions, prescription creams can be very effective, but results depend on the type of rash and the specific cream.

In women with vulvar lichen sclerosus, a very strong steroid cream (clobetasol 0.05%) relieved symptoms in about 75% of patients and clearly improved the skin under the microscope, outperforming testosterone, progesterone, or plain cream [5]. Clobetasol was also more effective than pimecrolimus, though both reduced itching and pain [7].

In boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans, a potent steroid (mometasone 0.05%) improved narrowing of the foreskin in many early and intermediate cases, whereas placebo often led to worsening [4].

By contrast, testosterone creams were no better than petrolatum (plain ointment) and did not improve tissue changes [3], and could even worsen symptoms when used after clobetasol [6]. Dihydrotestosterone improved skin appearance but not symptoms [1][2].

Because genital rashes have many causes, an accurate diagnosis and doctor‑chosen cream are essential.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles focus on specific chronic genital skin diseases—mainly lichen sclerosus of the vulva and balanitis xerotica obliterans of the foreskin. They compare different topical treatments, including strong steroid creams, hormone creams (testosterone/dihydrotestosterone), and plain emollient creams or placebo. Strong topical steroids (especially clobetasol) repeatedly show clear benefit in signs, symptoms, and tissue changes. Hormone creams show little benefit over simple ointments and can even worsen control when used after steroid therapy. A potent steroid also helps some boys with early foreskin disease. Because genital rashes have many causes and this skin is delicate, results support that certain prescription creams can be very effective when the diagnosis fits, but not all creams help, and medical guidance is essential.

- **Full Answer**:  
For some genital rashes, prescription creams do work, but the effect depends on the cause. In women with vulvar lichen sclerosus, very strong steroid creams like clobetasol clearly improved symptoms, skin appearance, and biopsy findings, and are considered first‑line treatment [5][7]. In contrast, hormone creams such as testosterone or dihydrotestosterone showed little or no symptom benefit and sometimes performed no better than plain ointment or even worse than simple emollient cream [1][2][3][6]. In boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans (a similar condition of the foreskin), a potent steroid cream improved tightness in many early or intermediate cases compared with placebo [4]. Overall, for specific inflammatory genital conditions diagnosed by a doctor, certain prescription topical creams—especially ultrapotent steroids—can be very effective, while many other creams provide only soothing moisture. Because genital skin is delicate and rashes have many causes, any strong or long‑term cream should be used only under careful medical supervision.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles focus on specific chronic genital skin conditions rather than all “rashes.” In women with vulvar lichen sclerosus and boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans, prescription topical creams—especially very strong steroid creams—were tested. The data show clear benefit from potent steroids, but little or no extra benefit from hormone (testosterone-type) creams compared with simple moisturizers. Because genital rashes can also be infections or other problems, a medical diagnosis is essential before using strong creams.

- **Full Answer**:  
Some genital “rashes” do respond well to topical creams, but success depends on the exact condition and type of cream.  

In vulvar lichen sclerosus, an ultra‑potent steroid cream (clobetasol 0.05%) relieved itching and pain in about 75% of women and clearly improved the skin, making it the preferred treatment [5][7]. Hormone creams with testosterone or dihydrotestosterone changed the skin’s appearance only modestly and often did not improve symptoms; symptoms could even worsen when testosterone was used after steroids [1][2][3][6]. Simple moisturizers such as petrolatum helped symptoms about as much as testosterone creams [3][6].  

In boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans, a potent steroid cream (mometasone 0.05%) improved or stabilized the condition compared with placebo [4].  

Because genital rashes have different causes, see a clinician before using these medicines.

- **Rationale**: Two randomized, placebo-controlled trials in people with severe kidney injury tested thyroid hormone treatment. In kidney transplant patients with delayed graft function from acute tubular necrosis, triiodothyronine (T3) did not improve dialysis needs, recovery time, hospital stay, or 1‑year kidney function versus placebo [1]. In general acute renal failure, thyroxine similarly failed to improve recovery or dialysis needs and was linked to higher death rates [2].

- **Full Answer**: Based on these studies, thyroid medicine is not an effective treatment for kidney damage when the thyroid is otherwise normal. Adding thyroid hormone after a kidney transplant with delayed function did not speed kidney recovery, reduce the need for dialysis, shorten hospital stay, or improve long‑term kidney function [1]. In people with acute kidney failure, thyroxine also did not improve any kidney outcomes and was associated with a higher risk of death [2]. Therefore, routine thyroid hormone therapy for kidney damage is not recommended.

- **Rationale**:  
Two high‑quality clinical trials tested thyroid hormones in people with severe, sudden kidney problems. One study gave T3 (a thyroid hormone) to kidney transplant patients with delayed function from acute tubular necrosis; it showed no benefit on dialysis needs, speed of recovery, hospital stay, or 1‑year kidney function [1]. Another study gave T4 (thyroxine) to patients with acute renal failure; it also showed no kidney benefit and found higher death rates in the thyroid‑treated group [2]. These results mean thyroid medicine does not repair kidney damage in people whose thyroid is otherwise normal and may even be dangerous.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies in people show that standard thyroid hormones do not help the kidneys recover from serious, sudden injury.  

In patients with delayed kidney function after a transplant due to acute tubular necrosis, giving triiodothyronine (T3) did not reduce the need for dialysis, speed kidney recovery, shorten hospital stay, or improve 1‑year kidney function compared with placebo [1].  

In patients with acute renal failure from various causes, giving thyroxine (T4) also did not change how many people needed dialysis, how many recovered kidney function, or how quickly they recovered [2]. In this study, death rates were actually higher in the thyroxine group (43%) than in the placebo group (13%), and the risk was linked to stronger thyroid‑stimulating hormone (TSH) suppression [2].  

Overall, thyroid medicine is not an effective treatment for kidney damage in people who have normal thyroid function, and it may even be harmful [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if thyroid medicine helps kidney damage. The articles summarize two good-quality clinical trials where thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) were given to people with serious kidney injury: delayed graft function after kidney transplant and acute renal failure. Both trials were randomized, placebo-controlled, and double-blind, which are strong designs. In both, thyroid hormone did not improve kidney recovery or outcomes and, in one, was linked to higher death rates. So, in people without true thyroid disease, thyroid medicine does not act as a kidney treatment and may be harmful.

- **Full Answer**:  
Current research does not support using thyroid hormone medicine to treat kidney damage.

In people with delayed function of a transplanted kidney caused by acute tubular necrosis, giving triiodothyronine (T3) did not improve the need for dialysis, speed of recovery, length of hospital stay, or kidney function at one year compared with placebo [1]. This means thyroid hormone did not help the injured kidney heal after transplant.

In a separate trial of patients with acute renal failure, thyroxine (T4) also failed to improve kidney recovery, need for dialysis, time to recovery, or survival [2]. In fact, death rates were higher in the thyroxine group, and the worse outcomes were linked to stronger thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) suppression from the drug [2].

These studies suggest thyroid hormone should not be used to treat kidney failure in people who have normal thyroid function. It may even be harmful in serious kidney illness [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is whether thyroid hormone medicines can treat kidney damage. Two good-quality clinical trials tested this in people whose thyroid levels were already in the normal “sick euthyroid” range. One trial used T3 in patients with delayed kidney function after a transplant, and another used thyroxine in people with acute kidney failure. Both compared thyroid hormone to placebo and looked at kidney recovery, dialysis needs, hospital stay, and survival. Neither study showed benefit; one showed higher death rates with thyroxine. This means that, outside of true thyroid disease, thyroid hormone should not be used as a treatment for kidney injury.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, standard thyroid medicines (like thyroxine or T3) do not help kidney damage when thyroid function is already normal.  

In people with delayed kidney function after a cadaver kidney transplant caused by acute tubular necrosis, adding T3 did not change the need for dialysis, speed of recovery, length of hospital stay, or kidney function at one year [1].  

In patients with acute renal failure, thyroxine likewise did not improve the chances of kidney recovery, reduce the need for dialysis, shorten the illness, or lower death rates [2]. In fact, death was higher in the thyroxine group and was linked to stronger suppression of thyroid-stimulating hormone [2].  

These results suggest thyroid hormone should not be given just to “boost” recovery from kidney injury in people who are not truly hypothyroid. Treatment should focus on the underlying kidney problem and regular supportive care, closely guided by a kidney specialist.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well surgery works for long‑lasting nose problems in children. The relevant surgery here is adenoidectomy (removal of adenoids). One randomized trial in very young children with recurrent ear infections found adenoidectomy did not prevent future infections or reduce doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with symptoms, compared with placebo or long‑term preventive antibiotics, so it was not recommended as first‑line surgery [1]. Another controlled study in children with long‑standing nasal blockage found that both operated and non‑operated children improved over time; surgery gave only a small, short‑term extra improvement in nasal blockage and no benefit for colds or ear infections [2]. Overall, benefits are limited and often temporary, while many children improve as they grow.

- **Full Answer**:  
Two studies looked at adenoidectomy, a common throat surgery in young children [1][2].  

In children 10–24 months old with frequent ear infections, adenoid removal did not reduce the number of future ear infections, doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with respiratory symptoms compared with placebo or long‑term preventive antibiotics [1]. The researchers concluded it should not be the first surgical choice for this problem [1].  

In another study of children with long‑standing nasal blockage, both operated and non‑operated children improved over time. Surgery produced a slightly greater reduction in moderate nasal blockage in the first year, and no difference by the second year [2]. It did not decrease colds or ear infections [2].  

Overall, for long‑lasting nose problems like nasal obstruction and recurrent ear infections in young children, adenoid surgery offers, at most, modest short‑term relief of blockage and no clear long‑term benefit, while symptoms often gradually improve naturally [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well surgery works for children with long‑lasting nose problems. The available articles focus on adenoidectomy (removal of adenoids), a common surgery for chronic nasal blockage and related ear problems. One good-quality randomized trial in very young children with recurrent ear infections found no real benefit from adenoidectomy compared with medicines or placebo over two years. Another controlled study in children with long‑standing nasal blockage showed only a small, short‑lived improvement in nasal obstruction after surgery, with no benefit for colds or ear infections. Together, these studies suggest limited and mostly temporary benefit.

- **Full Answer**:  
Two studies looked at adenoidectomy, a common nose and throat surgery in young children with long‑lasting ear and nose problems.  

In one large trial of children under 2 with frequent ear infections, having the adenoids removed did not meaningfully reduce new ear infections, doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with respiratory symptoms over two years compared with long‑term antibiotics or placebo [1]. The authors concluded it should not be the first treatment choice for these problems [1].  

In another study of children with serious, long‑standing nasal blockage, both the surgery group and the no‑surgery group improved over time. Surgery led to a slightly greater reduction in nasal blockage in the first year, but this advantage disappeared by the second year. Surgery did not lower colds or ear infections compared with no surgery [2].  

Overall, adenoid surgery offers at most small, mostly short‑term benefits for chronic nose problems in children [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
Two controlled studies looked at adenoidectomy (removing adenoid tissue behind the nose) in children with long‑lasting nose and ear problems. In very young children with repeated ear infections, surgery was directly compared with long‑term preventive antibiotics and with placebo. In another trial, children with long‑standing nasal blockage were randomly assigned to surgery or no surgery and followed for up to two years. Across both studies, surgery gave little or no long‑term benefit for infections, and only a small, short‑lived improvement in nasal blockage. Both operated and non‑operated children tended to improve over time, suggesting that many symptoms get better on their own as children grow. Overall, the research does not support adenoid surgery as a reliable long‑term solution for chronic nose or related ear problems in children, though it might still be considered in selected severe cases after discussion with a specialist.

- **Full Answer**:  
Two studies looked at removing the adenoids (glands high in the throat behind the nose) in children with long‑lasting nose and ear problems.  

In young children (10–24 months) who kept getting ear infections, adenoid removal did not lower the number of ear infections, doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with respiratory symptoms compared with medicine or placebo [1]. So surgery did not work as a long‑term prevention method [1].  

In older children with long‑standing blocked nose, both the surgery group and the non‑surgery group improved over time. Nasal blockage was slightly better in the surgery group during the first year, and there was no difference by the second year [2]. Surgery also did not reduce colds or ear infections [2].  

Overall, adenoid surgery gives at most a small, short‑term improvement in nasal blockage and does not help long‑term ear or infection problems, so it is not a reliable long‑term solution [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about whether surgery helps children with long‑lasting nose problems. The articles focus on adenoidectomy (removal of adenoids), a common nose/throat surgery in kids. One high‑quality trial in very young children with frequent ear infections found that adenoidectomy did not reduce future infections or related problems compared with medicine or placebo [1]. Another controlled study in children with long‑standing nasal blockage found only a small, short‑term improvement in blockage after surgery, and no lasting benefit for colds or ear infections [2]. Together, these suggest that adenoid surgery offers limited, mainly short‑term symptom relief and is not usually effective as a broad, long‑term solution for chronic nose problems in children.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, surgery to remove the adenoids (adenoidectomy) gives little long‑term benefit for most long‑lasting nose and ear problems in young children.  

In children 10–24 months old with repeated ear infections, adenoidectomy did not prevent new infections, doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with respiratory symptoms compared with long‑term preventive antibiotics or placebo [1]. Because it did not clearly reduce future problems, the authors do not recommend adenoidectomy as the first treatment in these cases [1].  

In another study of children with long‑standing nasal blockage, both children who had surgery and those who did not got better over time. Surgery slightly reduced moderate nasal blockage during the first year, but this small difference disappeared by the second year [2]. Surgery did not lower the number of colds or ear infections [2].  

Overall, adenoid surgery offers at best modest, often temporary relief, and is usually not a first‑line solution.

- **Rationale**:  
Only one study directly included womb (endometrial) cancer when comparing different hospitals. It found that, after accounting for age, stage of disease, and other health factors, treatment in large or teaching hospitals did not significantly improve overall survival for gynecologic cancers, including womb cancer [1]. Short‑term survival (30–90 days) was a bit better in high‑volume centers, but this did not translate into a clear long‑term survival advantage [1]. The most important factors were how advanced the cancer was and whether women received appropriate surgery and chemotherapy [1]. For ovarian cancer, several studies show better outcomes in specialized centers and with gynecologic oncologists [2–5], suggesting that expertise and quality of surgery matter, even if “special hospital” status alone is not the key driver.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research looking at womb (endometrial) and gynecological cancers found that being treated in large or teaching hospitals did not clearly improve overall survival once age, stage, and other factors were taken into account [1]. Patients at high‑volume centers were more likely to survive the first 30–90 days after treatment for cervical, ovarian, and womb cancers, but this advantage disappeared in longer‑term analyses [1]. Instead, the strongest factors linked to better survival were being younger than 65, having earlier‑stage disease, and receiving surgery and chemotherapy when appropriate [1]. For ovarian cancer (a different but related cancer), care in specialized cancer centers and by gynecologic oncologists is associated with better surgery quality, fewer repeat operations, and longer survival [2–5]. Taken together, this suggests that for womb cancer, expert surgical and oncology care is crucial, but simply attending a “special” or high‑volume hospital by itself has not been proven to improve survival [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about “special hospitals” (high‑volume or teaching centers) for womb (endometrial) cancer. Only one summary directly includes endometrial cancer and compares outcomes by hospital type and volume.[1] That study found an early survival benefit in high‑volume centers, but this disappeared after adjusting for patient age, stage, race, and treatment, meaning hospital type alone did not clearly improve longer‑term survival.[1] Other summaries focus on ovarian cancer, where specialization often helps, but those results cannot reliably be generalized to womb cancer.[2–5] Therefore, the answer must emphasize the mixed, limited evidence for womb cancer specifically.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research that included women with womb (endometrial) cancer compared outcomes between high‑volume or teaching hospitals and other hospitals.[1] At first glance, women treated in high‑volume centers had slightly better survival in the first 1–3 months after treatment.[1] However, when researchers took into account age, cancer stage, race, and treatments given, there was no clear long‑term survival advantage from being treated in a special or high‑volume hospital.[1] Instead, the strongest factors linked to survival were: being younger than 65, having cancer caught at an earlier stage, and actually receiving surgery and chemotherapy when appropriate.[1] Other studies in ovarian cancer suggest benefits from specialized surgeons and cancer centers, but these results cannot be assumed to apply in the same way to womb cancer.[2–5] Overall, current evidence suggests that treatment in special hospitals for womb cancer is not clearly more effective by itself; the quality of staging, surgery, and follow‑up treatment matters most.[1]

- **Rationale**:  
Only one study specifically included womb (endometrial) cancer along with other gynecologic cancers and compared outcomes by hospital type and volume [1]. In simple (“unadjusted”) comparisons, high‑volume centers showed slightly better short‑term survival for womb cancer, but this advantage disappeared once age, stage, and other factors were taken into account [1]. Survival was mainly driven by how advanced the cancer was at diagnosis and whether women received surgery and chemotherapy, not by whether the hospital was a teaching or high‑volume center [1]. Several other studies in ovarian cancer do show better results in specialized, higher‑volume centers and with gynecologic cancer specialists [2,3,4,5]. Because this clear benefit has not been demonstrated for womb cancer, the safest conclusion is that timely, appropriate treatment is more important than the label of the hospital.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research that directly looked at womb (endometrial) and other gynecologic cancers compared outcomes in high‑volume or teaching (“special”) hospitals with other hospitals [1]. At first glance, women with womb cancer treated in high‑volume centers seemed to have slightly better short‑term survival, but once researchers took into account age, stage of cancer, and other health factors, there was no meaningful difference in overall survival based on hospital volume or teaching status [1]. Instead, the strongest factors linked to better survival were being diagnosed at an earlier stage and actually receiving surgery and, when appropriate, chemotherapy [1]. Studies of ovarian cancer show clearer benefits from specialized centers and gynecologic cancer surgeons, including better surgery and survival [2,3,4,5], but this has not been clearly demonstrated for womb cancer. So, for womb cancer, getting timely, guideline‑based treatment may matter more than whether the hospital is officially a “special” or high‑volume center [1].

- **Rationale**:  
Only one article directly includes womb (endometrial) cancer and compares outcomes by hospital type/volume [1]. It initially showed better short‑term survival in high‑volume centers, but this advantage disappeared after adjusting for age, stage, and treatment, meaning hospital “specialness” alone did not clearly improve long‑term survival [1]. Other studies, although focused on ovarian cancer, consistently show that specialized centers and gynecologic oncologists achieve better surgery quality and survival [2–5]. Because the same specialist teams often treat both ovarian and womb cancers, it is reasonable to think that expertise and organized cancer pathways matter, even if the large womb‑cancer analysis did not prove a strong independent hospital‑type effect.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research directly looking at “special” or high‑volume hospitals for womb (endometrial) cancer is limited. In a large Florida study of several gynecologic cancers, people with womb cancer treated in high‑volume centers had slightly better 30–90‑day survival at first glance, but after carefully adjusting for age, stage, and treatment, long‑term survival did not clearly differ by hospital type or workload [1]. Instead, survival was strongly linked to being younger than 65, having earlier‑stage disease, and actually receiving surgery and chemotherapy when needed [1]. Studies in ovarian cancer, another gynecologic cancer treated by the same specialists, show that specialized centers and gynecologic oncologists achieve more thorough surgery and better survival [2–5]. This suggests expert teams and good care pathways matter, even if hospital “label” alone was not decisive for womb cancer in the Florida data [1]. Overall, timely diagnosis and high‑quality surgery remain the most important factors for long‑term chances of cure.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles focus on follicular lymphoma, a common slow‑growing blood cancer. Standard chemo‑immunotherapy (like CHOP‑R) and more intensive, transplant‑based approaches can put most patients into remission. In newly diagnosed high‑risk patients, intensive high‑dose treatment achieved more complete remissions (85% vs 62%) and better 4‑year disease control (61% vs 28%), but did not lengthen overall survival, partly because relapse could be successfully treated later (85% complete remission with salvage high‑dose therapy) [1]. In relapsed disease, high‑dose therapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant clearly improved both time without progression and survival compared with standard treatment (4‑year survival 71–77% vs 46%) [2]. When used as first treatment, transplant lengthened time without progression (9‑year 64% vs 39%) and may cure some patients, but did not improve overall survival and increased secondary cancers [4][5]. Overall, treatments are effective at long‑term disease control, and many patients live for years.

- **Full Answer**:  
For follicular lymphoma, a type of blood cancer, current treatments are quite effective at controlling the disease, often for many years.  

Standard chemotherapy plus rituximab puts most patients into remission; more intensive high‑dose chemotherapy with stem‑cell support produces even more complete remissions (85% vs 62%) and better 4‑year disease control (61% vs 28%), but overall survival is similar because relapses can often be successfully retreated [1].  

In relapsed follicular lymphoma, high‑dose therapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant significantly improves both progression‑free and overall survival compared with standard chemotherapy (4‑year survival about 71–77% vs 46%) [2].  

Using transplant as the very first treatment can lengthen the time before the cancer returns (9‑year progression‑free survival 64% vs 39%) and may cure a subgroup, but does not clearly extend overall survival and increases the risk of secondary cancers [4][5].  

So, treatment is effective at long‑term control, and many patients live a long time.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles focus on follicular lymphoma, a common blood cancer. They compare standard chemotherapy with more intensive, high‑dose treatments plus stem cell transplant. I summarized how often remissions occur, how long people stay cancer‑free, overall survival, and important risks, then translated this into clear language about effectiveness.

- **Full Answer**:  
For follicular lymphoma, standard chemo plus targeted drugs (like rituximab) puts most people into remission, but many eventually relapse [1,5]. Adding very intensive chemotherapy and an autologous stem cell transplant can deepen remission and keep the cancer away longer. For example, high‑dose treatment improved the chance of being progression‑free at 2–4 years from about 25–40% to around 55–64% [1,2,4,5]. In some patients, a long “plateau” without progression suggests possible cure [4].  

However, this more aggressive approach does not consistently lengthen overall survival compared with good standard therapy [1,4,5], partly because high‑dose therapy works well as a “salvage” option when the cancer comes back [1,2]. It also carries higher risks, including more secondary cancers [4].  

Overall, treatments are effective at controlling follicular lymphoma for many years, and survival is generally long, but the disease often behaves as a chronic condition rather than being reliably cured [1,2,4,5].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study follicular lymphoma, a common blood cancer. Standard chemotherapy plus the antibody rituximab leads to many complete remissions [1]. More intensive high‑dose chemotherapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant can further improve initial disease control and delay progression [1,4]. In patients whose lymphoma has come back, high‑dose therapy with transplant clearly improves how long people stay in remission and likely improves survival compared with standard chemotherapy alone [2]. However, giving such intensive treatment to everyone at diagnosis does not consistently make people live longer overall and increases risks such as second cancers [1,4–5]. Because effective high‑dose “salvage” treatment can be used if the lymphoma returns, many studies recommend reserving transplant for relapse rather than first‑line treatment [1,5]. Overall, these data show that treatment often works well at controlling follicular lymphoma for many years, and a subset of patients may be effectively cured [2,4].

- **Full Answer**:  
Many treatments do work for follicular lymphoma, a common slow‑growing blood cancer. In first‑line treatment, standard chemo plus rituximab puts most patients into complete remission (about 60%), and more intensive high‑dose chemo with stem‑cell support raises this to about 85% and doubles the number whose disease is undetectable with sensitive tests [1]. However, this added intensity does not make people live longer overall, partly because effective “salvage” high‑dose treatment can still be given if the lymphoma returns [1]. In relapsed lymphoma, high‑dose therapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant clearly prolongs time without progression and may improve survival compared with more standard chemo [2]. Using transplant up front can lengthen the first remission and may cure a subset, but it also increases the risk of serious second cancers and does not clearly extend overall survival [4–5]. Overall, many people live for years, often decades, with modern treatments [1–2,4–5].

- **Rationale**:  
Research in follicular lymphoma, a common blood cancer, shows several points. Standard chemotherapy plus antibody therapy (rituximab) gets many patients into complete remission, and even those who relapse can often respond again to more intensive treatment [1]. High‑dose chemotherapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant can roughly double the time patients live without their lymphoma worsening, and in some studies improves overall survival, especially when used after relapse [2,3,4]. However, using such very intensive treatment as the first therapy does not clearly help people live longer and can cause more serious long‑term side effects, including second cancers [1,4,5].

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For follicular lymphoma and related blood cancers, standard chemo‑immunotherapy can put most people into remission and control the disease for years [1,5]. If the lymphoma returns, high‑dose treatment with stem‑cell transplant can further lengthen survival and, for some, may be curative, though with higher risks [2,3,4]. Choice of timing remains an individual decision.

- **Rationale**: Studies looked at diuretics (medicines that increase urine) in premature babies with chronic lung disease/bronchopulmonary dysplasia. Short courses of furosemide or thiazide‑type drugs clearly increased urine and shifted water out of lung tissues, and several trials showed better lung mechanics and lower oxygen needs while babies were taking the drugs [1,3,6]. However, some regimens did not improve breathing despite more urine [2,5]. Benefits generally disappeared after stopping treatment and did not shorten time on oxygen or in hospital, though one trial found better survival to discharge [3,4]. Diuretics also caused salt and mineral losses [6].

- **Full Answer**: For long‑lasting lung disease in premature infants, pee‑making medicines can give modest, temporary help. While infants take certain diuretics, their lungs often become more compliant and airway resistance falls, so they may need less oxygen [3,4,6]. These gains fade after stopping, and treatment rarely shortens ventilator or oxygen days, though survival may improve somewhat [3,4].

- **Rationale**:  
Across several studies in premature infants with chronic lung disease, diuretic medicines (that increase urination) sometimes improved short‑term lung function and oxygen needs, but benefits were inconsistent and did not clearly shorten the overall course of the disease. Some combinations also caused side effects like salt and mineral loss.  

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines that make you pee more (diuretics) can help some premature babies with long‑lasting lung disease, but they are not a cure.

Short‑term, certain diuretic combinations improved how easily the lungs expand and how much resistance there is in the airways [3][4][6], and sometimes reduced the amount of oxygen babies needed while on the drug [3]. In one study, long‑term treatment also increased the chance of surviving to hospital discharge [4].  

However, other trials showed no improvement in breathing or oxygen levels despite more urine [2][5]. Even when lung function improved, babies did not get off oxygen or out of the hospital sooner, and the benefits faded after stopping the medicine [3][4].  

Diuretics can also cause losses of important minerals like potassium and phosphorus and affect growth [1][6].  

Overall, they offer modest, temporary help for symptoms, with important side effects, rather than a strong long‑term fix.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies in premature infants with long‑lasting lung disease (bronchopulmonary dysplasia/chronic lung disease) show mixed results with diuretics (medicines that increase urination). Several trials found better lung function and lower oxygen needs while babies were on certain diuretic combinations, but benefits often disappeared after stopping and did not clearly shorten hospital or oxygen time. Other trials showed no lung benefit despite more urine. Diuretics can also disturb salt and mineral balance, so they require monitoring. Overall, these medicines can help some infants breathe easier in the short term, but they are not a cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
In premature infants with chronic lung disease, diuretics move water out of the tissues and into the circulation, reducing excess fluid without changing overall body water or weight [1].  
Some diuretic combinations improved lung mechanics and reduced oxygen needs while the baby was taking them [3,4,6], and one study suggested better survival [4].  
However, other regimens (like adding spironolactone, or short‑term spironolactone–hydrochlorothiazide) increased urine but did not improve lung function or oxygen levels [2,5].  
Benefits generally did not persist after stopping the drugs and did not clearly shorten time on oxygen or in hospital [3,4].  
Diuretics can cause losses of important minerals such as potassium and phosphorus, so babies must be monitored closely [6].  

Overall, medicines that make you pee more can give some short‑term breathing benefits in this kind of long‑lasting lung disease in premature infants, but effects are limited and not permanent [1–6].

- **Rationale**:  
Studies in premature infants with chronic lung disease (bronchopulmonary dysplasia) tested “water pills” (diuretics). Some trials found that certain diuretic combinations improved lung function tests and reduced the amount of oxygen babies needed while they were on the medicine [3,4,6]. One study even showed better survival to hospital discharge with long‑term treatment [4]. However, other studies found no improvement in breathing or oxygen levels even though urine output increased [2,5]. In several trials, benefits disappeared after the medicine was stopped and did not shorten the total time on oxygen or in hospital [3,4]. Diuretics can also cause side effects like loss of important salts and minerals and slower weight gain [6]. Simply making babies pee more and lose fluid does not always translate into better lung function [1,5].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines that make you pee more (diuretics) can sometimes help certain babies with long‑lasting lung disease, but they are not a cure.  

In premature infants with bronchopulmonary dysplasia, some diuretic combinations (like chlorothiazide plus spironolactone) improved breathing tests and reduced how much oxygen the babies needed while they were taking the drugs [3,4,6]. One study also found more babies survived to hospital discharge with long‑term diuretic therapy [4].  

However, other trials showed no improvement in lung function or oxygen levels despite higher urine output [2,5], and benefits often disappeared after stopping the medicine, without reducing total days on oxygen or in the hospital [3,4]. Diuretics can also cause loss of important salts and minerals, and may slow early weight gain [6].  

Overall, diuretics may be useful for selected infants with chronic lung disease, but simply increasing urine output does not always improve lung function, and treatment must be individualized and closely monitored [1–6].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well bariatric (weight loss) surgery treats liver swelling from fat, which is non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and its more severe form, NASH. Across many studies of gastric bypass, banding, gastroplasty, duodenal switch, and biliopancreatic diversion, liver biopsies before and after surgery show consistent patterns: large, sustained weight loss is usually followed by big reductions or disappearance of liver fat, frequent resolution of NASH, and partial improvement of scarring. Modern procedures generally help the liver when nutrition is adequate; older or highly malabsorptive operations can be riskier. Insulin resistance strongly influences who fully recovers. I therefore describe effectiveness in terms of proportions improving/resolving steatosis, inflammation, and fibrosis, and note exceptions and long‑term patterns.

- **Full Answer**:  
Weight‑loss (bariatric) surgery is usually very effective for “fatty, swollen liver” caused by obesity (non‑alcoholic fatty liver disease).

Across multiple studies, 70–90% of patients lost about half of their excess weight and had major drops in liver fat: steatosis improved or disappeared in most people after gastric bypass, banding, or gastroplasty, often within 1–2 years [1][3][4][8][10][11][13][15][20]. In studies focused on steatohepatitis (fat plus inflammation), biopsy‑proven NASH resolved in about 80–90% of patients after gastric bypass or similar surgery [4][7][13][15][20]. Liver scarring (fibrosis) usually improved or stayed stable; about one‑third to one‑half of patients had less fibrosis, and worsening was uncommon when nutrition was adequate [1][3][4][9][13][16][20][21]. Very malabsorptive operations or poor protein intake can harm the liver, so standard modern procedures are preferred [1][12][21]. People whose insulin resistance stays high are more likely to retain liver fat [5][16][19]. Overall, surgery greatly improves fatty liver for most, but not all, patients overall.

- **Rationale**:  
Across many studies of people with severe obesity who had weight‑loss surgery, doctors took liver biopsies before and after surgery. These showed how much fat, inflammation, and scarring were present. Most patients started with fatty liver or NASH (fat plus inflammation). After losing a large amount of weight, liver fat and inflammation usually fell sharply, and often disappeared. Scarring (fibrosis) often improved but more slowly, and a small group did not fully improve, especially those with very advanced damage. Improvement was strongest in people who lost more weight and whose insulin resistance got better. I summarized and translated these findings into lay terms.

- **Full Answer**:  
Modern weight‑loss surgeries (such as gastric bypass, banding, and duodenal switch) are very effective at reducing fat‑related liver swelling (fatty liver and NASH) in people with severe obesity. In multiple studies where doctors took liver biopsies before and after surgery, 70–90% of patients had major improvement in liver fat, and fat completely disappeared in about half to more than 80% of patients.[1][3][4][10][11][13][20] In people with NASH (fat plus inflammation), clear resolution of NASH occurred in roughly 80–90% after one to three years.[4][7][13][15] Scarring (fibrosis) often improves or stabilizes, but tends to change more slowly than fat and inflammation; most patients end up with only mild or no scarring, though a small minority may not improve.[3][4][9][10][16][21] The better and more sustained the weight loss and insulin resistance improvement, the better the liver response.[9][16][19] Very advanced cirrhosis may not fully reverse, so early treatment is best for long‑term liver health and survival.[17][21]

- **Rationale**:  
Across many studies of people with severe obesity, bariatric operations (such as Roux‑en‑Y gastric bypass, sleeve-like procedures, banding, and duodenal switch) caused large, sustained weight loss and were followed by clear improvement in fatty liver. Most patients had much less fat in the liver, and many had complete normalization on repeat biopsy within 1–3 years [1][3][4][8][11][13][14][16]. In those with more serious damage (nonalcoholic steatohepatitis and fibrosis), inflammation and scarring often improved or resolved, though fibrosis may persist or even worsen in a minority, especially with ongoing insulin resistance or nutritional problems [3][4][6][7][9][15][17][18][21]. Older operations that caused extreme malabsorption had more liver risk [1][21], but modern procedures generally did not show progression to cirrhosis and often reversed early scarring [3][4][13][15][18][21]. Overall, surgery is not a cure-all, but for appropriately selected patients with obesity-related fatty liver, it is one of the most effective treatments.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. In people with severe obesity, weight‑loss (bariatric) surgery usually works very well for “liver swelling from fat” (fatty liver). In multiple studies, most patients had major reductions in liver fat, and many had a completely normal liver on biopsy within 1–3 years after gastric bypass or gastroplasty [1][3][4][8][11][13][14][16].  

For those with more advanced disease (nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, or NASH), surgery‑induced weight loss often led to disappearance of NASH in 70–90% of patients and clear improvement in liver inflammation and early scarring (fibrosis) [3][4][6][7][13][15][18]. Some scarring may persist, and a minority show stable or slightly worse fibrosis, especially if insulin resistance remains high or nutrition is poor [9][17][21].  

Older, highly malabsorptive operations carried more risk, but modern bariatric procedures generally improve, rather than worsen, fatty liver and related damage when done in appropriate candidates and followed closely [1][12][19][21].

- **Rationale**:  
Across many studies where liver biopsies were done before and after bariatric (weight‑loss) surgery, doctors directly measured changes in fat, inflammation, and scarring in the liver. Most involved modern procedures such as Roux‑en‑Y gastric bypass, banding, gastroplasty, or duodenal switch, in people with severe obesity and fatty liver disease. I focused on whether liver fat and swelling improved, whether the more serious form (non‑alcoholic steatohepatitis, NASH) got better, and what happened to liver scarring. I also checked for reports of harm or worsening liver damage.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For people with severe obesity, modern weight‑loss surgery usually improves liver swelling caused by fat (fatty liver). In multiple studies, fat in the liver fell sharply or disappeared in about 60%–90% of patients after large, sustained weight loss [1–4,8,10,11,13,15,16,18,20]. The more serious form, NASH, improved or resolved in roughly 80%–90% of patients [2–4,7,9,14,15,18,20]. Liver scarring (fibrosis) often became milder or stabilized, with complete remission in some, and worsening was uncommon [3,4,6,7,9–11,13,16,20–22]. Benefits were closely tied to better insulin resistance and metabolic health after surgery [3,5,16,19].  

Old intestinal bypass operations could harm the liver, but current standard procedures generally did not [1,12]. Surgery is not a quick fix: it works best with long‑term follow‑up, healthy eating, and medical monitoring.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well “brain medicines” work for thinking problems mainly due to poor blood flow (vascular causes). The articles all test one such medicine, rivastigmine. In a large trial of people with probable vascular dementia, rivastigmine slightly improved test scores on cognitive scales, but did not clearly help everyday functioning or behavior, and the benefit seemed limited to those likely also having Alzheimer’s disease, not pure vascular problems [1]. A study in Chinese patients with subcortical vascular dementia showed no clear cognitive or daily-function benefit [2]. A small trial in people with milder post‑stroke thinking problems found only modest improvement on one verbal thinking test and a trend on an attention test [3]. Side effects, especially nausea and vomiting, and higher withdrawal rates were common [1][2]. Overall, effectiveness appears small and uncertain.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines that boost brain chemicals, such as rivastigmine, have only modest and uncertain benefits for thinking problems caused mainly by poor blood flow to the brain (vascular dementia).

In a large 24‑week trial in people with probable vascular dementia, rivastigmine slightly improved test scores on several thinking scales compared with placebo, but it did not clearly help daily functioning or behavior overall [1]. The benefit seemed to come mostly from older patients who likely also had Alzheimer’s disease changes, not “pure” blood‑flow problems [1]. Another study in Chinese patients with subcortical vascular dementia found no meaningful improvement in memory, thinking, or daily tasks over six months [2].

In people with milder, post‑stroke thinking problems (cognitive impairment, no dementia), a small trial showed rivastigmine slightly improved one test of verbal thinking and possibly complex attention, but effects were limited [3].

Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, and higher dropout rates [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how well “brain function” medicine works for thinking problems caused by poor blood flow (vascular causes, including stroke and vascular dementia). The articles all study rivastigmine, a cholinesterase inhibitor used for memory. Across several trials in vascular dementia and post‑stroke cognitive problems, results are at best modest and inconsistent. Benefits, when present, are mostly on specific test scores, not on daily functioning, and may mainly occur in people who also have Alzheimer’s‑type brain changes. Some studies show no cognitive benefit at all, and side effects and dropouts are more common with the drug. So, overall, effectiveness appears limited and uncertain.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines that boost brain chemicals, such as rivastigmine, have shown limited benefit for thinking problems caused by poor blood flow (vascular disease).

In a large 24‑week trial of people with probable vascular dementia, rivastigmine slightly improved scores on several memory and thinking tests compared with placebo, but it did not improve daily functioning or overall clinical status [1]. The benefit seemed to come mostly from older patients who were more likely to also have Alzheimer’s disease changes, not “pure” blood‑flow problems [1]. In younger patients, no benefit was seen and there were concerns about higher blood pressure, strokes, and deaths [1].

A smaller study in Chinese patients with subcortical vascular dementia found no clear cognitive benefit and more dropouts on rivastigmine [2]. In people with milder post‑stroke thinking problems, a pilot trial suggested a modest improvement in one test of mental flexibility only [3].

Overall, effectiveness is modest and uncertain.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all examine rivastigmine, a drug used to support brain function in Alzheimer’s disease, in people whose thinking problems are mainly from poor blood flow (vascular dementia or post‑stroke cognitive impairment). Across studies, rivastigmine showed little or no clear benefit in most patients with vascular causes alone, with some side effects. Modest benefits mainly appeared in people who probably also had Alzheimer’s disease, not pure blood‑flow problems. A small post‑stroke study showed only limited improvement on specific tests. Overall, evidence does not support strong, consistent benefit for thinking problems caused primarily by poor blood flow.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines like rivastigmine, which boost brain chemicals, have been tested in thinking problems caused by poor blood flow to the brain (“vascular” cognitive problems).

In a large study of people with probable vascular dementia, rivastigmine slightly improved some thinking test scores, but did not help daily functioning or behavior, and benefits mainly appeared in older patients who likely also had Alzheimer’s disease, not pure blood‑flow problems [1]. Other, younger patients showed no benefit and had more strokes, higher blood pressure, nausea, vomiting, and some increased deaths [1]. A smaller study in Chinese patients with subcortical vascular dementia also found no meaningful cognitive benefit and more drop‑outs on rivastigmine [2].

In people with milder, non‑dementia thinking problems after stroke, a small pilot study showed only limited improvement on one verbal task and a trend in one executive test [3].

Overall, these medicines show at best modest, uncertain benefit for blood‑flow‑related thinking problems [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about drugs that “boost brain function” (like rivastigmine, used in Alzheimer’s disease) and whether they work when thinking problems are mainly from poor blood flow (vascular dementia or post‑stroke cognitive problems). In a large vascular dementia trial, rivastigmine only gave small test gains and mainly helped older people who probably also had Alzheimer’s changes, not those with “pure” blood‑flow disease [1]. A study in subcortical vascular dementia showed no clear cognitive benefit and more side‑effect drop‑outs [2]. A small trial in milder, post‑stroke cognitive impairment showed only modest improvement on one specific test [3]. Overall, evidence is weak and inconsistent for vascular causes alone.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines like rivastigmine are designed to boost brain chemicals and are approved for Alzheimer’s disease, not specifically for memory and thinking problems caused mainly by poor blood flow (vascular causes).  

In people with clear vascular dementia, rivastigmine gave small test-score improvements but did not clearly help everyday function or behavior; the benefits mainly appeared in older patients who likely also had Alzheimer’s disease, not “pure” blood‑flow problems [1]. In younger vascular dementia patients, there was no benefit and some signals of harm, such as higher blood pressure and more strokes and deaths [1]. Another trial in subcortical vascular dementia found no meaningful cognitive gains and more drop‑outs from side effects [2].  

A small study in people with milder post‑stroke thinking problems showed modest improvement on one word‑fluency test and was generally well tolerated [3].  

Overall, these medicines do not reliably help thinking problems from poor blood flow alone in the brain.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies in otherwise healthy children and teens looked at oral acyclovir, an antiviral medicine, started within 24 hours of the chickenpox rash. Across trials, acyclovir consistently shortened the illness and reduced the number and duration of skin spots and symptoms, but it did **not** clearly reduce serious complications. It appeared particularly useful in adolescents, who tend to have more severe chickenpox.

- **Full Answer**:  
The main medicine studied for chickenpox is the antiviral drug acyclovir, taken by mouth several times a day for about 5 days. When started within 24 hours of the rash, it modestly shortens the illness and makes symptoms less severe.

In children, acyclovir reduced the number of spots, sped up crusting and healing, and shortened fever and “feeling sick” by about a day compared with no medicine [2][3]. In teenagers, it clearly reduced the number of spots, fever duration, and leftover marks, with no extra side effects [1].  

However, in large studies, acyclovir did **not** clearly reduce rare serious complications (like severe skin infections or brain problems) [2][3].  

Overall, acyclovir is safe and somewhat helpful for faster recovery, especially in adolescents, but it is not a cure-all and doesn’t clearly prevent serious complications [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
Studies looked at acyclovir, an antiviral medicine, in otherwise healthy children and teens with chickenpox. All trials started treatment within 24 hours of the rash appearing. Across studies, acyclovir consistently shortened the time children had fever, stopped new spots forming sooner, and reduced the total number of spots and leftover marks [1–3]. However, acyclovir did not clearly reduce the already low rate of serious complications in healthy children [2,3]. Side effects were rare and similar to placebo [1–3].

- **Full Answer**:  
In healthy children, acyclovir usually makes chickenpox milder rather than “curing” it. When started in the first day of the rash, it modestly shortens illness: fever tends to end a day earlier, and spots stop appearing and start crusting sooner [1–3]. Children get fewer spots, less itching, and fewer marks left on the skin a month later [1–3]. It has not been proven to prevent serious complications in otherwise healthy children [2,3].

- **Rationale**: Healthy children and teens in studies were given the antiviral drug acyclovir or a placebo. When started within 24 hours of the chickenpox rash, acyclovir reduced how many spots appeared, how long new spots kept appearing, and how long fever and illness lasted, compared with no treatment [1–3]. Those who took acyclovir also healed faster and had fewer marks left on the skin later on [1,3]. However, these studies did not show a clear drop in serious complications, which were already uncommon [2,3]. Acyclovir was generally well tolerated and considered safe in these trials [1–3].

- **Full Answer**: Yes. A prescription antiviral medicine acyclovir can make chickenpox milder and shorter when it is started within 24 hours after the rash appears [1–3]. In healthy children and teens, it also leads to fewer spots, less itching, and faster healing and fever resolution [1–3]. It has not clearly been shown to prevent serious complications [2,3].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all test the antiviral drug acyclovir for chickenpox (varicella) in otherwise healthy children and adolescents. Across studies, starting acyclovir within 24 hours of the rash consistently made the illness milder and shorter: fewer spots, faster crusting/healing, and shorter fever and “flu‑like” symptoms [1][2][3]. It did not clearly reduce already rare serious complications, and the disease usually gets better on its own. Acyclovir was generally safe and did not harm long‑term immunity [1][2][3]. So medicine can help, but it is not mandatory for every healthy child.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes, an antiviral medicine called acyclovir can help chickenpox, but it doesn’t make it disappear instantly.  

In otherwise healthy adolescents, starting oral acyclovir within 24 hours of the rash shortened the time new spots appeared, reduced the total number of spots, lowered fever sooner, and left fewer marks a month later, compared with placebo [1]. Similar benefits were seen in school‑age children: acyclovir led to fewer spots, faster healing of the rash, and quicker relief of fever and general sick feelings, again when started in the first 24 hours [2][3].  

Across these studies, acyclovir was generally well tolerated and did not appear to interfere with long‑term immunity to chickenpox [1][2][3]. However, it did not clearly reduce the already rare serious complications of chickenpox [2][3].  

So, medicine can make chickenpox milder and shorter if started early, but it is not essential for every healthy child; a doctor should advise you individually.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies give mixed signals. An older 9‑year series found spinal cord stimulation (SCS) not clinically useful for cancer pain overall, though only 11 cancer patients were included and technology and techniques were earlier generation [2]. A large study of epidural spinal stimulation across many conditions showed that cancer (carcinoma/sarcoma) pain actually responded better than average, with most patients getting at least moderate relief [3]. More recent case reports show people with severe, well‑localized cancer‑related chest wall or low back pain achieving excellent and sustained (12‑month) relief after a successful SCS trial and implantation [1][4]. Overall, benefit appears possible but not predictable and evidence is limited.

- **Full Answer**:  
Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) can help some people with cancer pain, but its effectiveness is uncertain and seems to depend on the pain type and location.  

In one large study using epidural spinal stimulation, about 71% of patients across conditions had at least moderate pain relief, and cancer (carcinoma/sarcoma) pain actually responded better than average [3]. However, another series from an earlier era found “no clinical usefulness” of SCS for cancer pain in 11 patients [2].  

More recent case reports describe people with severe cancer‑related chest wall or low back pain who had “excellent” relief during a SCS trial and maintained strong pain control for at least 12 months after permanent implantation [1][4].  

Overall, SCS may offer long‑lasting relief for selected patients with localized, neuropathic‑type cancer pain that has not improved with medications, but results are variable and evidence is limited, so careful evaluation and a trial are essential [1][2][3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles give mixed evidence. Two recent case descriptions report excellent, long‑lasting pain relief from spinal cord stimulation (SCS) in patients with cancer‑related chest wall or low‑back pain after other treatments failed [1][4]. A larger study of many pain conditions found that cancer (carcinoma/sarcoma) pain was more likely than average to improve by at least half with epidural spinal cord stimulation [3]. However, an older series of 11 patients concluded SCS had no useful benefit for cancer pain overall [2]. These studies are small, use different techniques, and involve selected patients, so results can’t be generalized to all cancer pain.

- **Full Answer**:  
Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) can help some people with cancer‑related pain, but results are mixed and evidence is limited.  

Small reports of patients with chest wall or low‑back pain from cancer who already failed usual treatments describe “excellent” pain relief during the SCS trial and sustained benefit for at least 12 months after permanent implantation [1][4]. In a larger series using a related technique, about 70% of patients had at least 30% pain relief; people with carcinoma or sarcoma pain were more likely than average to achieve over 50% relief [3].  

However, an older study of 11 patients with cancer pain found SCS had “no clinical usefulness” for that group overall [2].  

Taken together, SCS seems most effective for carefully selected patients with localized, neuropathic‑type cancer pain that has not responded to medicines or other procedures, but it does not reliably help all cancer pain. Specialist assessment is essential.

- **Rationale**:  
Research on spinal cord stimulation (SCS) for cancer pain is mixed. An older study of many patients found SCS was not useful for cancer pain overall [2]. However, larger surveys using a different SCS technique reported that people with carcinoma or sarcoma more often achieved meaningful pain reduction than average, with most patients getting at least some relief and few serious complications [3]. More recent case reports show patients with cancer‑related chest wall or low‑back pain, whose pain had not improved with medications, achieving strong and lasting relief for at least 12 months after SCS implantation [1][4]. Overall, SCS is not a first‑line treatment and does not help everyone, but it can be an option for carefully selected patients with difficult, likely neuropathic cancer pain, after standard treatments have failed.

- **Full Answer**:  
Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) uses thin wires near the spinal cord to send mild electrical pulses that change pain signals.  

Evidence is mixed. In one older series, SCS did **not** appear helpful for cancer pain overall [2]. But a later study of 454 people found better‑than‑50% pain relief was **more common** in patients with carcinoma or sarcoma than in most other conditions, and serious complications were rare [3].  

Recent case reports show people with severe cancer‑related chest wall or low‑back pain, who did poorly on medications, gained excellent and lasting pain relief (up to 12 months) after SCS implantation [1][4].  

Taken together, SCS is **not guaranteed** to work and isn’t usually a first choice. However, for some carefully selected patients—especially with localized, nerve‑type cancer pain that has not responded to drugs—it can provide meaningful, sometimes major, pain relief [1][3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
Some reports show strong pain relief from spinal cord stimulation (SCS) in individual cancer patients, especially for chest wall and low‑back pain, with benefits lasting at least 12 months after implantation [1][4]. A larger study of many diseases found that people with cancer (carcinoma/sarcoma) were more likely than average to get at least 50% pain relief, often allowing reduction or stopping of pain medicines, and complications were rare [3]. However, an older series of patients found SCS was not clinically useful for cancer pain overall [2]. This mixed evidence suggests SCS can help some carefully selected patients, particularly when pain has a nerve‑related component and other treatments have failed, but it does not work for everyone.

- **Full Answer**:  
Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) can relieve cancer‑related pain for some patients, but results are mixed and it is not guaranteed to work.

Small case reports show excellent, lasting relief of cancer‑related chest wall pain and low back pain when SCS leads are placed at the appropriate spinal levels, with benefit maintained for at least 12 months after implantation [1][4].  

In a larger study of many pain conditions, people with cancer (carcinoma/sarcoma) were more likely than average to report more than 50% pain relief, often reducing or stopping pain medicines, and serious complications were uncommon [3].  

However, an earlier 9‑year experience in one center concluded that SCS was not clinically useful for cancer pain overall [2].  

Taken together, SCS may help selected patients with difficult cancer pain—especially when nerve pain is involved and standard treatments have failed—but it is not effective for all types of cancer pain [1][2][3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles compare common pain medicines after wisdom-tooth surgery. All three studies show that taking ibuprofen and paracetamol (acetaminophen) together works better than taking either medicine alone, and often better than combinations that include codeine. Patients on the ibuprofen–paracetamol combination had lower pain scores over 8–12 hours, faster onset of relief, longer-lasting relief, and were less likely to need extra “rescue” opioid tablets. Side effects (like nausea and dizziness) were similar or less frequent than with other pain combinations, including those containing codeine. These trials used single or as‑needed doses in people with moderate to severe pain, so they are a good indicator of how well these medicines work for short‑term post‑surgery pain, at least for dental surgery.

- **Full Answer**:  
For short‑term pain after dental surgery, pain medicines are clearly effective. All active pain medicines worked better than placebo [1][2][3].  
The best results came from taking ibuprofen plus paracetamol together. This combination gave stronger and longer pain relief over 8–12 hours than the same doses of either ibuprofen or paracetamol alone [1][3], and was comparable or superior to ibuprofen–codeine and paracetamol–codeine combinations [2].
