Across several studies of people with newly diagnosed focal/partial epilepsy, standard seizure medicines controlled seizures in a large share of patients. Carbamazepine, a common first-choice drug, generally worked as well as or better than vigabatrin, a newer option, though each had different side-effect patterns. Around half of adults became completely seizure‑free on either carbamazepine or vigabatrin in some trials, and many others had major reductions in seizure frequency. In children, both drugs often led to very good control (more than 75% fewer seizures), with only a minority seeing little or no benefit. Some people who did not respond to one medicine improved when switched or when medicines were combined. Overall, these data suggest that, for newly diagnosed focal seizures, medicine alone can greatly reduce or stop seizures for most patients, though not for everyone, and choice of drug may affect both effectiveness and tolerability.

- **Full Answer**:  
In newly diagnosed focal (partial) epilepsy, seizure medicines work well for many people.  

In adults, about half became completely seizure‑free on carbamazepine or vigabatrin in one study [2]. Another large trial found carbamazepine controlled seizures better than vigabatrin, with longer time before seizures returned and better overall seizure outcomes, though both allowed many patients to achieve at least 6 months without seizures [1]. Over 12 months, about 60% of adults stayed on their initially assigned drug with acceptable control and side effects [4].  

In children, most had large improvements. Over 75% seizure reduction was seen in 85% of those on vigabatrin and 61% on carbamazepine, with only a few children worsening or not improving [3]. A 2‑year study also found similar seizure control with both medicines [5].  

Some people who did not respond to one drug improved when switched or when drugs were combined [2]. Overall, medicines substantially reduce or stop seizures for a majority, but not all, patients.

- **Rationale**:  
  I used results from several randomized and comparative studies of two standard seizure medicines, carbamazepine and vigabatrin, in adults, teens, and children with newly diagnosed partial (focal) epilepsy. These trials report how many people became seizure‑free or had large seizure reductions, how long they could stay on the same medicine, and what happened when medicines were combined. I translated the percentages into simple terms (like “about half” or “most”) to make the information clearer for non‑specialists, while keeping the key numbers and clearly citing each statement.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for seizure disorders are often quite effective, but not perfect.  

In adults and teens with newly diagnosed focal (partial) epilepsy, about 60% could stay on their initial medicine for a full year with good control of seizures using either carbamazepine or vigabatrin [4]. Among those who responded, more carbamazepine‑treated patients became completely seizure‑free than those on vigabatrin [1,4]. In another adult study, complete seizure control was achieved in about half of patients on either drug (46% with vigabatrin, 51% with carbamazepine) [2].  

In children with partial seizures, 61–85% had a very large reduction in seizures (over 75% fewer) on either medicine over months to years [3,5]. However, a small number had no benefit or even worse seizures, and some needed both drugs together, which helped some previously resistant cases [2,3].  

Overall, seizure medicines greatly reduce or stop seizures for most—but not all—people with epilepsy [1–5] over the long term.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles compare two seizure medicines, carbamazepine and vigabatrin, in adults and children with newly diagnosed epilepsy. Across several randomized and long‑term studies, a large share of patients had major seizure reduction or complete seizure control on these medicines [1][2][3][4][5]. Around half of adults became seizure‑free on each drug in some studies, and most children had at least a 75% reduction in seizures [2][3][5]. About 60% of adults stayed on their first prescribed medicine for at least a year with acceptable control and side effects [4]. Not everyone responded, and side effects sometimes required switching drugs [1][2][3][4][5].

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for seizure disorders (epilepsy) work for many people, reducing or stopping seizures.  

In large studies of people with newly diagnosed partial (focal) seizures, standard medicine carbamazepine and the newer drug vigabatrin each gave complete seizure control in about half of adults [1][2] and in most children treated, often with more than 75% seizure reduction [3][5]. Over 12 months, about 60% of adults stayed successfully on their assigned drug, with acceptable seizure control and side‑effects [4]. When one medicine did not fully control seizures or caused side‑effects such as rash, mood or behavior changes, or weight gain [1][2][3][4][5].  

However, no medicine works for everyone: some people continued to have seizures or stopped a drug because of side‑effects such as rash, mood or behavior changes, or weight gain [1][2][3][4][5].  

Overall, the evidence shows that anti‑seizure medicines are effective and necessary, but treatment must be individualized and monitored by a specialist [1]–[5].

- **Rationale**:  
Across several studies, standard anti-seizure medicines (like carbamazepine and vigabatrin) reduced or stopped seizures in a large proportion of people with newly diagnosed epilepsy. In adults, about half or more became seizure‑free on one medicine, and others still had fewer seizures, though some stopped the drug because of side effects or lack of effect [1][2][4]. In children, most had very good seizure reduction (over 75%) on these medicines [3][5]. No medicine worked for everyone, and each had different side effects, but overall they clearly improved seizure control compared with no treatment.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Medicines for seizure disorders (epilepsy) help many people have far fewer seizures, and often none.  

In adults with newly diagnosed focal seizures, carbamazepine and vigabatrin both controlled seizures in many patients; about half achieved complete seizure control on each drug in one study [2], and carbamazepine was somewhat more effective than vigabatrin in another large trial [1]. About 60% of adults stayed successfully on their initial medicine for a year [4].  

In children with new partial seizures, most had very good seizure control (over 75% fewer seizures) on either vigabatrin or carbamazepine [3][5].  

However, not everyone becomes seizure‑free, and some people stop a drug because of side effects or poor control [1][2][4]. Different medicines suit different patients, so doctors often adjust treatment over time. Overall, anti‑seizure medicines are effective for many, but not all, people with seizure disorders [1][2][3][4][5].

- **Rationale**:  
Studies of capsaicin 0.075% cream in long‑lasting nerve pain (neuropathy, shingles pain, postsurgical pain) generally show moderate benefit, though one trial in distal polyneuropathy found no advantage over placebo. In several conditions, 50–80% of patients report at least some pain relief, often around 40–50% average reduction, with improved sleep and daily activities. Benefits usually appear over several weeks and can be maintained long term. The main side effect is burning or stinging where it’s applied.

- **Full Answer**:  
For long‑lasting nerve pain, capsaicin cream helps many, but not all, patients. In painful diabetic neuropathy, about 70–76% of people using 0.075% cream had pain improvement over 8 weeks, with average pain intensity cut by more than 40% and better sleep and walking [1][4]. In chronic shingles pain (postherpetic neuralgia), roughly 80% had some relief after 6 weeks [2], and benefits could be maintained or increased for up to 2 years in 86% of patients [5]. Capsaicin also reduced chronic postsurgical nerve pain by about 53% versus 17% with placebo [7] and helped many women with postmastectomy pain [6].  

However, one 12‑week study in chronic distal polyneuropathy found no clear benefit over placebo [3]. The most common side effect is local burning or stinging, usually without serious problems [1][5][7].

- **Rationale**:  
Multiple randomized trials of 0.075% capsaicin cream in chronic nerve pain (from diabetes, shingles, surgery, and mastectomy) show moderate pain reduction for many, but not all, patients. In diabetic neuropathy, capsaicin works about as well as a standard nerve-pain pill, with fewer body-wide side effects [1][4]. In long‑lasting postherpetic neuralgia and postsurgical neuropathic pain, a majority get meaningful relief, sometimes sustained for years [2][5][7]. However, one study in distal polyneuropathy found no clear benefit over placebo [3]. The main problem is local burning or stinging, especially early on, but serious harms are rare [5][7].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Capsaicin cream (usually 0.075%) can give meaningful relief for many people with long‑lasting nerve pain, but it does not help everyone.  

In diabetic nerve pain, about 70–76% of patients using capsaicin for 8 weeks reported less pain, with average pain cut by more than 40%, similar to the benefit from the pill amitriptyline but with fewer whole‑body side effects [1][4]. In long‑standing shingles nerve pain (postherpetic neuralgia), roughly 80% had some relief after 6 weeks, and most who kept using it up to 2 years maintained or improved their benefit [2][5]. Studies in post‑surgical and post‑mastectomy nerve pain also showed about half or more patients getting at least 50% pain reduction [6][7]. However, one trial in painful polyneuropathy found no clear advantage over placebo [3].  

Common issues are burning or stinging where it’s applied, especially early on, but serious side effects are rare [5][7]. Discuss this option with your clinician.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at capsaicin cream (0.075%) in several kinds of chronic nerve pain lasting months to years. Most trials in diabetic neuropathy, long‑standing postherpetic neuralgia, and postsurgical neuropathic pain show moderate but meaningful pain reduction versus placebo, with improved sleep and daily function. One study in chronic distal polyneuropathy did not find benefit over placebo, so it does not work for everyone or every nerve condition. Long‑term data up to 2 years suggest benefits can be maintained. The main side effect is local burning or stinging at the application site.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research shows that capsaicin cream can help many people with long‑lasting nerve pain, but the benefit is usually moderate and not guaranteed.  

In painful diabetic nerve damage, capsaicin 0.075% used four times daily for 8 weeks reduced pain and improved sleep and daily activities, working about as well as the oral drug amitriptyline, but with far fewer body‑wide side effects [1][4]. In people with shingles nerve pain lasting more than 6–12 months, capsaicin gave clearly better pain relief and function than inactive cream, and benefits often continued or improved with up to 2 years of ongoing use [2][5]. It also helped some women with chronic post‑mastectomy pain and people with persistent postsurgical cancer‑related nerve pain [6][7]. However, one trial in another type of chronic polyneuropathy did not find capsaicin better than placebo [3]. The main downside is local burning or stinging where it is applied, initially, for many patients [5][7].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about long-lasting (chronic) nerve pain. The articles describe several randomized, controlled trials of capsaicin 0.075% cream in different chronic neuropathic pain conditions. For painful diabetic neuropathy, two studies show clear benefit versus placebo and similar benefit to amitriptyline, while one high-quality study did not find capsaicin better than placebo, indicating mixed but overall supportive evidence [1][3][4]. For long-standing postherpetic neuralgia, two trials show significant benefit over placebo, and one shows that benefits can be maintained or increased for up to two years [2][5]. Smaller studies in postmastectomy and postsurgical neuropathic pain also show meaningful pain reductions [6][7]. Across studies, the main side effect is local burning or stinging, usually manageable [5][7]. Overall, evidence supports capsaicin cream as a reasonable, relatively safe option for some people with chronic nerve pain, though it does not help everyone and pain relief is typically moderate.

- **Full Answer**:  
Capsaicin cream can help some people with long‑lasting nerve pain, but it does not work for everyone, and benefit is usually moderate.  

In diabetic nerve pain in the feet, several 8–12‑week trials found capsaicin 0.075% used four times daily reduced pain and improved walking, sleep, and daily activities, working about as well as the oral drug amitriptyline, but with fewer body‑wide side effects [1][3][4]. However, one careful study did not find capsaicin better than placebo, suggesting a strong placebo effect and variable response [3].  

For long‑lasting shingles pain (postherpetic neuralgia), two randomized trials showed significantly more pain relief with capsaicin than placebo, and benefits could be maintained or increased for up to two years of continued use [2][5]. Smaller studies also suggest benefit in post‑mastectomy and postsurgical neuropathic pain [6][7].  

The main downside is burning or stinging at the application site, especially early in treatment [5][7], often improving over time.

- **Rationale**:  
Randomized trials in women with early-stage ovarian cancer compared giving chemotherapy right after surgery with either no immediate treatment or other older treatments. A large modern trial (ICON1) found that platinum-based chemotherapy after surgery both reduced recurrences and modestly improved 5‑year survival. Older cisplatin trials also showed fewer recurrences but no clear survival benefit at 5 years. In very low‑risk women who were carefully and completely staged, survival was already excellent, and adding older chemotherapy (melphalan) did not clearly help. One small trial of carboplatin in higher‑risk stage I disease was inconclusive. Overall, the benefit of chemotherapy is largest for women with higher‑risk features (e.g., higher grade, capsule rupture, spread beyond the ovary), while some low‑risk women can safely skip it if staging is complete.

- **Full Answer**:  
Chemotherapy after surgery can lower the chance that early ovarian cancer will come back and may slightly improve survival, but the benefit depends on risk level.  

In a large modern trial, women who received platinum‑based chemotherapy after surgery had about 11% fewer recurrences and about 9% higher 5‑year survival (79% vs 70%) than those who waited for chemotherapy until relapse [4]. Earlier cisplatin studies also showed a roughly 60–65% reduction in recurrences, but no clear survival advantage at 5 years [1].  

For the lowest‑risk women (small, stage I tumors, well‑ or moderately‑differentiated, fully staged), survival without chemotherapy was already very high (over 90–95%), and adding melphalan chemotherapy did not clearly improve outcomes [2][3].  

Some smaller trials of carboplatin in higher‑risk stage I disease did not show a clear benefit, but they were too small to be definitive [5].  

Overall, chemotherapy is most recommended for women with higher‑risk early disease.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles look at women with early-stage (mostly stage I–II) epithelial ovarian cancer who had surgery and were randomly assigned to get chemotherapy or not. A large modern trial (ICON1) showed that platinum-based chemo after surgery improved both survival and time without recurrence. Older and smaller trials with different drugs or small sample sizes sometimes did not show clear survival benefits, but some did show big reductions in relapse risk. Very low‑risk women (small, well‑behaved tumors confined to the ovary and completely staged) already had excellent outcomes without chemo. Because many women are not fully staged and some hidden spread can be missed, guidelines lean toward offering chemo to all but the lowest‑risk group. Overall, adjuvant chemo gives a modest but real survival benefit on average, with the biggest absolute gain in women at higher risk of recurrence.

- **Full Answer**:  
After surgery for early-stage ovarian cancer, chemotherapy mainly helps by lowering the chance the cancer comes back. In one large trial, platinum-based chemotherapy after surgery improved 5‑year survival from about 70% to 79% and reduced recurrences from 38% to 27% [4]. Other studies using cisplatin showed a 60–65% drop in relapse risk, although an overall survival advantage was less clear [1]. Older drugs like melphalan or internal radiation (32P) gave about 80% of higher‑risk patients 5‑year disease‑free survival, but did not clearly beat each other [2]. One carboplatin study also failed to show a clear benefit, partly because it was small [5]. Very low‑risk women (small, well‑differentiated tumors confined to the ovary and fully staged) did very well without chemo, with over 90% disease‑free at 5 years [2]. Because many women are not fully staged at surgery, doctors often recommend chemo for all but the lowest‑risk patients in practice [3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
Randomized trials in women with early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer have tested whether giving chemotherapy after surgery (adjuvant chemo) lowers the chance of the cancer coming back and improves survival. Older Italian trials found that cisplatin after surgery clearly reduced relapses by about 60–65%, but they could not show a clear survival benefit, partly because relapsed patients who had not received chemo could still be treated later [1]. Trials using older drugs (melphalan, intraperitoneal 32P) showed that some very low‑risk patients did just as well without chemo, while higher‑risk patients had about 80% 5‑year disease‑free survival with adjuvant treatment [2]. A large, modern international trial (ICON1) using platinum-based chemotherapy showed a real survival benefit: 5‑year survival improved from 70% to 79% and the risk of recurrence also fell (62% to 73% disease‑free) [4]. One smaller high‑risk stage I trial with carboplatin was inconclusive because of too few patients [5].

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, chemotherapy after surgery can help many women with early ovarian cancer, but it is not necessary for everyone.  

Platinum-based chemotherapy (like cisplatin or carboplatin) after surgery clearly lowers the risk that cancer will come back in early‑stage disease [1][4]. In older trials, cisplatin cut relapses by about 60–65%, although a clear survival benefit was not always seen [1].  

A large international trial (ICON1) found that giving platinum-based chemo after surgery improved 5‑year survival from 70% to 79% and reduced recurrences (62% to 73% disease‑free) [4]. This shows that, on average, adjuvant chemotherapy does work for early epithelial ovarian cancer.  

However, some very low‑risk women (cancer confined to the ovaries, well‑differentiated, carefully staged) did just as well without chemo in older studies using melphalan [2]. A small study in high‑risk stage I patients using carboplatin was inconclusive but showed no clear difference, likely due to limited size [5].  

Decisions now depend on risk features and how completely surgery was staged [2][3][5].

- **Rationale**:  
Chemotherapy after surgery (“adjuvant chemo”) has been tested in several trials of early-stage ovarian cancer. Older studies using drugs like melphalan or intraperitoneal radiation (32P) showed little or no clear survival benefit, especially for low‑risk stage I patients, who already did very well with surgery alone [1][2][5]. More recent trials using platinum chemotherapy have shown modest reductions in cancer recurrence risk [1][4] and, in a large study, better overall survival over several years [4].

- **Full Answer**:  
For many women with early ovarian cancer, chemotherapy after surgery helps, but the size of the benefit depends on risk level. In one trial, platinum-based chemotherapy improved 5‑year survival from 70% to 79% and reduced recurrences from 38% to 27% [4]. Another trial of carboplatin showed identical survival with or without chemo, so benefit there was uncertain [5]. Well‑staged, low‑risk stage I patients may avoid chemo, as their survival is above 90% with surgery alone [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The evidence comes from three randomized controlled trials. In people with major depression on fluoxetine, adding low‑dose folic acid clearly improved outcomes in women but not men, and also reduced side effects [1]. In patients (depressed or schizophrenic) who were folate‑deficient, high‑dose methylfolate improved both symptoms and social functioning over six months [2]. In older adults with dementia and depression, a high dose of methylfolate worked about as well as the antidepressant trazodone over eight weeks [3]. Overall, folate seems most effective as an add‑on—especially when folate levels are low—not as a stand‑alone replacement for antidepressants.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies suggest folate can modestly improve depression, mainly as an add‑on to treatment.  

In people taking fluoxetine, adding 500 micrograms of folic acid daily led to greater symptom improvement than fluoxetine plus placebo, but this benefit was clear only in women: about 94% responded to treatment with folate versus 61% without it, and average depression scores were almost half as high at 10 weeks [1]. Side effects were also reported less often in the folate group [1].  

In patients with major depression or schizophrenia who had low folate levels, high‑dose methylfolate (15 mg/day) for six months improved both clinical symptoms and social functioning compared with placebo [2].  

In older adults with dementia and depression, 5‑methyltetrahydrofolate (50 mg/day) reduced depression scores to a similar extent as the antidepressant trazodone over eight weeks [3].  

Overall, folate appears helpful, especially when folate is low, but it should be used alongside—not instead of—antidepressants.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how effective folate supplements are for depression. The summaries provide three randomized, controlled trials. One tested regular folic acid added to fluoxetine, another used high‑dose methylfolate in patients with low folate, and a third used 5‑methyltetrahydrofolate in older adults with dementia and depression. Across studies, folate was used alongside standard psychiatric medications, not as the only treatment. I focused on changes in depression scores, response rates, and comparisons with antidepressants to explain effectiveness in clear, practical terms.

- **Full Answer**:  
Folate supplements appear modestly effective for depression, mainly as an add‑on to standard treatment rather than a stand‑alone cure.

In adults taking fluoxetine, adding 500 micrograms of folic acid daily led to greater symptom improvement than fluoxetine alone, especially in women: about 94% of women on folate had at least a 50% reduction in depression scores, versus 61% on placebo [1]. Side effects were not increased and may have been fewer with folate [1].

In patients hospitalized with major depression or schizophrenia who had low folate levels, high‑dose methylfolate (15 mg/day) improved both clinical symptoms and social functioning more than placebo over six months [2].

Among elderly people with dementia and depression, high‑dose 5‑methyltetrahydrofolate (50 mg/day) reduced depressive symptoms over eight weeks about as much as the antidepressant trazodone [3].

Overall, folate (especially methylfolate forms) may enhance antidepressant response, particularly when folate deficiency is present [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
Research suggests folate-related supplements can help depression, mainly as add‑ons to antidepressants or in people with low folate. One trial found folic acid improved response to fluoxetine, especially in women, and reduced side effects. Others showed high‑dose methylfolate or 5‑MTHF improved recovery or depressive symptoms, including in older adults, in some cases similar to an antidepressant. However, studies are small, use different forms and doses, and don’t show folate works alone. More research is needed.  

- **Full Answer**:  
Folate supplements appear to help people with depression, but mainly as an add‑on, not a replacement for antidepressants. Adding 500 micrograms of folic acid to fluoxetine led to greater improvement and higher response rates in women than fluoxetine alone, with fewer side effects [1]. In people with folate deficiency or elderly patients, high‑dose methylfolate (15 mg) or 5‑MTHF (50 mg) improved depressive symptoms and recovery alongside standard treatment [2][3]. Talk with a clinician before starting.

- **Rationale**:  
Research suggests folate (folic acid or methylfolate) can modestly help depression, mainly as an add‑on to standard treatment and particularly in people with low folate levels or in certain groups (e.g., women, older adults). However, the evidence comes from relatively small, specific studies and does not show folate as a stand‑alone cure.  

- **Full Answer**:  
Folate supplements can improve response to antidepressants in some people, but they are not a primary treatment for depression.  

In one study, adding 500 micrograms of folic acid to fluoxetine (an SSRI) led to greater improvement in depression, especially in women; almost 94% of women on folate plus fluoxetine had a good response versus 61% on fluoxetine alone [1].  

In patients with folate deficiency and major depression or schizophrenia, high‑dose methylfolate (15 mg/day) improved clinical and social recovery when added to usual medication [2].  

In elderly patients with depression and dementia, a folate form (5‑MTHF) reduced depressive symptoms about as well as the antidepressant trazodone [3].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies focus on people with severe leg artery blockage (critical limb ischemia) needing bypass surgery, often when a good vein is not available and artificial (PTFE) grafts must be used. Overall, they report how often the bypass stays open (“patency”) and how often the leg can be saved from major amputation (“limb salvage”) over 1–3 years. Different technical tweaks (vein collars/cuffs, arteriovenous fistulas, special graft designs) rarely made a large difference. One exception is a vein cuff improving results for certain below‑knee bypasses. Vein grafts generally perform better than prosthetic grafts but are not always possible.

- **Full Answer**:  
Bypass surgery for severely reduced leg blood flow can often prevent amputation, but the long‑term success of the graft is limited, especially when artificial (PTFE) tubes are used instead of the patient’s own vein.  

Across studies, 1–2‑year limb‑salvage rates are usually about 60–75% with PTFE bypasses to below‑knee or foot arteries [1–3,6]. When spliced vein is possible, limb salvage can be around 85–94% at 2 years [4].  

However, many grafts narrow or close: 2–3‑year primary patency rates are often only 20–50% for PTFE grafts to below‑knee or more distant arteries [1–3,6]. Technical additions like vein collars/cuffs or arteriovenous fistulas usually do not clearly improve outcomes, with one trial showing better 2‑year patency (52% vs 29%) and slightly higher limb salvage (84% vs 62%) for a cuff in below‑knee popliteal PTFE bypasses [5].  

Overall, surgery frequently saves the limb but may require close follow‑up and potential re‑interventions.

- **Rationale**:  
I focused on outcomes most important to patients: how often the bypass stays open (“patency”) and how often the leg is ultimately saved (“limb salvage”). I summarized across trials of people with very poor leg circulation (critical limb ischemia) treated mostly with plastic (PTFE) bypasses, and highlighted that using the patient’s own vein generally improves results. I avoided surgical jargon and gave rounded ranges that reflect the different studies.

- **Full Answer**:  
Surgery to bypass blocked leg arteries can often prevent amputation, but the new graft does not always stay open long term. When synthetic (plastic) grafts are used below the knee, only about 20–50% remain open after 2–3 years, depending on how far down the leg they go [1][2][3][6]. Despite this, about half to two‑thirds of legs are still saved from major amputation at 2–3 years with synthetic grafts in very sick patients [1][2][3][6]. Results are better when the body’s own vein can be used: limb salvage around 85–94% at 2 years, with higher long‑term graft function, although more reoperations may be needed [4][5]. Technical “add‑ons” like vein collars or arteriovenous fistulas usually do not give large or consistent benefits [1][2][3][6]. Overall, bypass surgery offers a good chance of keeping the leg, but many patients need close follow‑up, further procedures, or eventually face graft failure.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies all look at surgery to bypass blocked leg arteries in people with very poor blood flow (critical limb ischemia). They show that bypass surgery using either vein or synthetic (PTFE) grafts can keep the new vessel open (patent) in about half of patients at 1–3 years, and, more importantly, prevent amputation in many cases. Limb salvage rates generally range from about 60–75% with PTFE grafts [1][2][3][6] and up to 85–94% when suitable vein is available or used creatively (spliced vein) [4]. Technical add‑ons like vein collars, cuffs, or arteriovenous fistulas usually do not make a big difference, except that a vein cuff improved results for below‑knee PTFE bypasses in one trial [1][5]. So, surgery is not perfect and sometimes needs re‑operations, but it often improves blood flow enough to relieve pain, heal wounds, and avoid major amputation.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For people with severely reduced leg blood flow (critical limb ischemia), bypass surgery can meaningfully improve outcomes and often prevent amputation.  

Across multiple trials of bypass using synthetic grafts (PTFE), about half of the grafts stay open at 1–3 years, but limb salvage is higher—typically around 60–75% [1][2][3][6]. When surgeons can use your own veins, including spliced vein segments, leg‑saving rates at 2 years can reach 85–94% [4].  

Many technical “extras” (vein collars, cuffs, or added arteriovenous fistulas) do not consistently improve results, although a vein cuff at the below‑knee popliteal artery did increase 2‑year patency (52% vs 29%) and tended to improve limb salvage (84% vs 62%) in one study [5]. Other collar or fistula additions showed no clear benefit [1][3][6].  

Overall, surgery cannot guarantee success, but it often restores enough flow to relieve symptoms, heal wounds, and avoid major leg amputation [1][2][4][5][6].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if surgery “works” for poor leg blood flow, meaning: does bypass surgery keep arteries open and prevent amputation. The trials summarize outcomes in patients with very severe disease (critical limb ischemia). I focused on two key outcomes: graft patency (how often the bypass stays open) and limb salvage (keeping the leg). I compared results for vein grafts versus synthetic PTFE grafts and looked at whether technical modifications (vein cuffs/collars, arteriovenous fistulas) truly improved success.

- **Full Answer**:  
Surgery to bypass blocked leg arteries can work, especially when blood flow is so poor that amputation is a risk. In people with severe “critical limb ischemia,” bypass grafts using the patient’s own veins give the best long‑term results and can keep the leg in about 85–95% of cases over two years, though further procedures are sometimes needed [4]. When good veins are not available and plastic (PTFE) grafts must be used, results are more limited: only about 30–50% of these grafts stay open at 2–3 years, but they still prevent amputation in roughly 60–70% of patients [1][2][3][5][6]. Technical additions such as vein “cuffs,” “collars,” or arteriovenous fistulas change results only modestly or not at all [1][2][3][5][6]. Overall, leg bypass surgery often improves blood flow enough to relieve pain and heal wounds, but durability varies and many patients need close follow‑up and occasional re‑operations [1][2][4][5][6].

The question is about chronic (long‑lasting) bone infection, or chronic osteomyelitis. Across multiple randomized trials and series, researchers used long courses (about 4–8 weeks) of antibiotics, often together with surgery to remove infected or dead bone. Oral fluoroquinolones (such as ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin, levofloxacin, lomefloxacin) generally performed about as well as standard intravenous regimens when the bacteria were susceptible [1][2][3][4][6][8]. Reported long‑term success (no signs of infection, healed wounds, and no relapse on follow‑up) typically ranged from about 60–80%, sometimes higher in selected patients [1][2][3][4][6][8]. Failures were often due to relapse within a year, particularly with difficult bacteria like Pseudomonas or mixed infections [2][3]. Combination therapy (e.g., nafcillin plus rifampin for Staphylococcus aureus) may improve outcomes for some [5]. Side effects occurred but were usually mild and reversible [1][2][3][4][7][8].

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotics can work, but they are not perfect for long‑lasting bone infections (chronic osteomyelitis).  

In several studies where people were treated for about 4–8 weeks, infection was cured or “arrested” (no signs of infection and healed wounds) in roughly 60–80% of patients, sometimes well over 85–90% in selected groups [1][2][3][4][6][8]. Relapse (the infection coming back within months to a year) still happened in about 15–40% of cases, especially when hard‑to‑treat germs like Pseudomonas or multiple bacteria were present [2][3].  

Oral antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin worked about as well as standard intravenous (IV) antibiotics when the bacteria were sensitive [1][2][3][4][6][8]. Combining certain drugs, like nafcillin plus rifampin for staph infections, may improve results for some patients [5].  

Most side effects were mild and reversible [1][2][3][4][7][8].  

Surgery to remove dead bone plus a long course of antibiotics generally offers the best chance of controlling or curing chronic bone infection [3][6].

- **Rationale**:  
Research on chronic (long‑lasting) bone infection (osteomyelitis) shows that antibiotics are helpful but not perfect. When the bacteria are sensitive to the chosen drug and dead or infected bone is surgically cleaned out, most—but not all—people can be kept free of infection for years. In multiple randomized trials, oral antibiotics (mainly fluoroquinolones like ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin) worked about as well as standard intravenous regimens, with broadly similar cure or control rates and few serious side effects [1–4,6,8]. However, certain bacteria, especially Pseudomonas and mixed gram‑negative infections, were much harder to clear and relapsed more often [2,3,7]. For Staphylococcus aureus, combining nafcillin with rifampin may improve success, but data are limited [5]. Overall, long courses of antibiotics are important and often effective, yet relapses still occur, meaning careful follow‑up and sometimes repeat surgery are necessary.

- **Full Answer**:  
Across multiple studies of chronic (long‑lasting) bone infection, 60–85% of adults treated with antibiotics plus appropriate surgery stayed infection‑free for many months to several years [1–4,6–8]. In randomized trials, oral fluoroquinolone tablets (such as ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin) were about as effective as standard intravenous regimens, with similar cure rates of roughly three‑quarters of patients [1–4,8]. One study of different fluoroquinolones found cure in 60–71% of patients overall, but only 40% with ciprofloxacin in that series [6]. Adding rifampin to nafcillin for Staphylococcus aureus bone infection improved success from 4/8 to 8/10 patients, though the study was small and not statistically conclusive [5]. Certain germs (especially Pseudomonas and mixed gram‑negative infections) were much harder to eradicate and failed or relapsed more often [2,3,7]. Overall, long courses (4–8 weeks or more) of appropriately chosen antibiotics are often effective, but relapse remains fairly common, so ongoing monitoring is essential [1–8].

- **Rationale**:  
Long-lasting bone infection (chronic osteomyelitis) is hard to treat but not hopeless. Multiple trials show that several weeks of antibiotics can stop the infection in most patients, especially when the bacteria are sensitive to the drug and dead bone is surgically cleaned out. Success rates are usually around 60–85%, but relapses and failures still happen, especially with certain germs like Pseudomonas. Oral antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin can work as well as intravenous drugs when organisms are susceptible, making treatment more convenient.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For chronic bone infections, antibiotics often work, but they usually need to be given for many weeks and combined with surgery to remove infected tissue. Oral drugs like ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin were about as effective as standard intravenous antibiotics, with cure or control in roughly 70–80% of patients [1][2][3][4][8]. Other studies show long-term success rates of about 60–86% with appropriate antibiotic choices and prolonged treatment [3][5][6][7]. However, some patients relapse, especially with difficult bacteria such as Pseudomonas [2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
Across multiple trials of chronic (long‑lasting) bone infection, prolonged antibiotic treatment helped most patients, though not all. Oral antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin, levofloxacin and others worked about as well as standard intravenous (IV) antibiotics when the germs were susceptible and patients often also had surgical cleaning of the bone. Long‑term cure or control rates generally ranged from about 60% to 86%, but some patients relapsed, especially with harder‑to‑treat bacteria like Pseudomonas or mixed infections. Side effects occurred but were usually mild and manageable.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For chronic bone infection (chronic osteomyelitis), weeks of antibiotics can control or cure the infection in most—but not all—patients.  

Studies show similar success with oral antibiotics (like ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin) and standard IV antibiotics after surgery to clean the bone, with long‑term success in roughly 60–86% of patients [1][2][3][4][6][8].  

However, some people relapse within a year, especially with Pseudomonas or mixed infections [2][3]. Cure rates in controlled trials vary but are often around three‑quarters of patients or higher [2][3][4][5][7][8].  

So, antibiotics do work for many chronic bone infections, but treatment must be long, targeted to the specific bacteria, often combined with surgery, and followed closely for relapse [2][3][5][6].

The studies look at a special type of breathing machine called high‑frequency jet ventilation (HFJV) in premature babies with respiratory distress. One small study found no clear benefit over standard ventilators for death or major lung problems [1]. A larger study, when HFJV was used with a careful lung‑protective strategy, showed fewer cases of chronic lung disease and less need for oxygen at home [2]. Another study, however, found more serious brain injuries and poor outcomes in babies started early on HFJV [3]. Overall, HFJV supports breathing as well as standard machines, but its extra benefits and risks depend heavily on how and when it is used.

- **Full Answer**:  
Breathing machines (ventilators) are often lifesaving for premature babies with serious breathing trouble.  

Special “jet” ventilators work about as well as standard ventilators for short‑term survival and basic lung problems; early use did not clearly lower deaths, air leaks, or chronic lung disease in one trial [1] and showed no lung advantage in another [3]. In a larger study of relatively stable preterm babies, jet ventilation, when used with a careful “optimal lung volume” strategy, cut the rate of chronic lung disease at 36 weeks (20% vs 40%) and reduced the need for oxygen at home [2].  

However, a different study found more serious brain injuries (cystic periventricular leukomalacia and severe bleeding or death) in babies started early on jet ventilation [3].  

Overall, ventilators are essential, but this special jet type is not clearly better than standard machines and may carry extra risk unless used with very strict protocols [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how effective a breathing machine is for babies with breathing trouble. All three articles study a specific type of breathing machine called high‑frequency jet ventilation (HFJV) in very premature babies with respiratory distress syndrome. One trial found no clear benefit over standard ventilators for survival or major lung problems [1]. A larger trial showed HFJV, used with an “optimal lung volume” strategy, reduced chronic lung disease and need for oxygen at home, with no extra short‑term risks [2]. Another trial, using a different strategy, found more serious brain injury with HFJV [3]. So HFJV can be effective, but results depend heavily on how it is used.

- **Full Answer**:  
Mechanical breathing machines, including high‑frequency jet ventilators, are effective at keeping very premature babies with serious lung disease alive by taking over some or all of the work of breathing. In the studies summarised here, this type of ventilator worked about as well as standard ventilators for short‑term outcomes like survival, air leaks, and bleeding in the brain [1][2][3]. One large study found that, when used with a specific “optimal lung volume” strategy, high‑frequency jet ventilation led to less long‑term lung damage (bronchopulmonary dysplasia) and fewer babies needing oxygen at home, without extra short‑term complications [2]. However, another study using a different strategy found more serious brain injury and poor outcomes with this ventilator compared with standard ventilation [3]. Overall, breathing machines are essential and usually effective, but the exact type and settings matter and must be carefully chosen and monitored by specialists [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies all involve very sick premature babies with serious breathing problems who needed a breathing machine (ventilator). In every trial, both regular ventilators and high‑frequency jet ventilators were able to support breathing and keep babies alive at similar rates, showing that ventilators do “work” for breathing failure. However, the special high‑frequency jet ventilators were not consistently better and sometimes carried extra risks.

- **Full Answer**:  
Breathing machines (ventilators) do help babies with serious breathing trouble by taking over or supporting their breathing while their lungs recover. In several studies of premature babies with respiratory distress syndrome, both standard ventilators and high‑frequency jet ventilators gave similar survival and basic breathing support [1][2][3].  

One large trial found that high‑frequency jet ventilation reduced long‑term lung damage and the need for oxygen at home, without increasing overall complications, when used with specific settings [2]. But another study showed that early use of high‑frequency jet ventilation increased the risk of serious brain injury and poor outcomes, with no clear lung benefit [3].  

So, ventilators do work for breathing trouble in babies, but the exact type and settings matter, and doctors balance benefits and risks for each baby [1][2][3].

The studies look at a special type of breathing machine called high‑frequency jet ventilation (HFJV) for premature babies with respiratory distress. - **Full Answer**:  
Breathing machines (ventilators) are standard, life‑saving treatment for newborns who cannot breathe well on their own, such as premature babies with respiratory distress syndrome. In all the studies, babies were able to maintain oxygen and carbon dioxide levels using mechanical ventilation [1][2][3].  

For the special type called high‑frequency jet ventilation (HFJV), results are mixed. One trial found no improvement in survival or major lung problems compared with a regular ventilator [1]. Another large study showed that, when used with carefully managed “optimal” settings, HFJV reduced long‑term lung disease and the need for oxygen at home, without increasing other complications [2].  

However, a different study using another HFJV strategy found more serious brain injury and poor outcomes compared with standard ventilation [3].  

So, yes—breathing machines do work to treat serious breathing trouble in babies, but the specific type and settings must be chosen and closely managed by specialists.

Across multiple randomized controlled trials, women with severe low‑back labour pain who received small injections of sterile water into or under the skin of the lower back consistently reported larger pain reductions than women given saline placebo. The effect appears within about 10 minutes and can last up to 1–2 hours. The injections sting briefly but no serious side‑effects or harms to mother, baby, or labour progress were reported. However, the method mainly eases back pain (not all contraction pain), and some women still need additional pain medicines.

- **Full Answer**:  
Sterile water injections into the skin of the lower back give meaningful short‑term relief of low‑back labour pain for many women.  

Several randomized trials comparing sterile water with saline “placebo” found pain scores fell much more after sterile water. Typical reductions were about 4–5 cm on a 10‑cm pain scale at 10–45 minutes, versus about 1 cm with placebo [2][3][4][6][7]. The benefit can last about 1–2 hours in many women [1][4][5][7]. Women often still feel contraction pain, but the intense back pain is less.  

The injections themselves sting or burn briefly, but no serious side‑effects, problems in labour progress, or harm to the baby were reported [1][2][5][7]. The method did not clearly reduce the need for additional drugs in all studies [4].  

Overall, sterile water injections are a simple, low‑cost option that can moderately reduce low‑back labour pain for a few hours, especially where epidurals are unavailable [1][2][3][5][6][7] in labour.

- **Rationale**:  
Multiple randomized controlled trials compared sterile water injections in the lower back with saline placebo for labour-related low back pain. All showed significantly greater pain reduction with sterile water, typically by about 4–5 cm on a 10‑cm scale versus about 1–2 cm with placebo, with effects lasting up to 1–2 hours. No important side effects or adverse effects on labour or the baby were reported. However, some women still needed additional pain medication. Overall, the evidence supports sterile water injections as a safe, low‑cost method that provides moderate, short‑term relief of severe low back pain in labour, especially where other options (like epidurals) are limited.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies show that small injections of sterile water into the skin or just under the skin of the lower back can meaningfully reduce labour-related low back pain for many women. Compared with saline (placebo), women who received sterile water typically reported pain scores falling by about 4–5 cm on a 10‑cm visual scale within 10–45 minutes, while placebo lowered pain by only about 1–2 cm [2–4,6]. The benefit can last at least 45–120 minutes in many women [1,2,4,5,7]. The injections themselves sting or burn briefly, but no serious side effects for mother, baby, or labour progress were reported [1,2,4–7]. Some women still required opioid medication, so this method may not fully replace other analgesia [4]. Overall, sterile water injections are a simple, low‑cost option that can provide moderate, short‑term relief of severe low back labour pain, especially useful where epidurals or other drugs are unavailable or undesired [1–7].

- **Rationale**:  
Several randomized controlled trials compared small injections of sterile water under or into the skin of the lower back with placebo (saline) during the first stage of labour. Across different countries and techniques, women who received sterile water consistently reported greater reduction in low‑back labour pain for about 45–120 minutes, without harming the baby or slowing labour. The method mainly targets severe continuous back pain, not all types of labour pain, and may sting briefly when injected. It is low‑cost and simple, but does not provide the same broad pain relief as an epidural.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Small injections of sterile water into or under the skin over the lower back significantly reduce low‑back labour pain compared with saline placebo in multiple trials [1–7]. Women who received sterile water reported much larger drops in pain scores at 10–45 minutes, often lasting up to 1–2 hours [1–3,5–7]. Labour progress, delivery method, and baby outcomes were similar between water and placebo groups, suggesting the treatment is safe for mother and baby [1,4,7]. The main drawback is brief but intense stinging when the water is injected [2,4]. This technique mainly helps severe continuous low‑back pain in the first stage of labour and does not replace epidural anaesthesia for overall labour pain, but it is a simple, low‑cost option where other methods are unavailable or undesired [1–7].

- **Rationale**:  
Several randomized controlled trials compared small injections of sterile water into the skin or just under the skin of the lower back with placebo (saline) in women in early labour who had severe low‑back pain. Across studies, women who received sterile water consistently reported much larger and clinically meaningful reductions in back pain for about 30–120 minutes, while labour progress and baby outcomes were unchanged. The main downside was a brief stinging sensation at the time of injection. This method targets continuous back pain, not all contraction pain, so it does not fully replace options like epidurals but can be a useful, low‑tech option, especially where epidurals are unavailable.

- **Full Answer**:  
Multiple studies show that small injections of sterile water into the skin of the lower back can reduce low‑back labour pain. Women in labour who received sterile water injections reported much larger drops in pain scores than those given saline (placebo) at 10–90 minutes, and up to 2 hours after treatment [1][2][3][4][5][6][7]. Both intracutaneous (just under the skin) and subcutaneous (a bit deeper) injections worked similarly well [2][3][5][6][7]. The injections sting or burn for a short time, but no serious side effects, problems with labour progress, or harm to the baby were found [1][2][4][5][7].  

This method mainly helps severe continuous low‑back pain in the first stage of labour; it does not replace an epidural for strong contraction pain throughout labour. Overall, for women who have back labour and either cannot or do not want an epidural, sterile water injections are a simple, low‑cost option that provides short‑term pain relief [1]–[7].
